review_instructions: Use only the declared Core Value and displayed Journal Entry text, including any displayed nudge and response.
Mark yes only when the writer describes their own directly observable behavior or choice against the declared Core Value.
Mark no for aligned or neutral behavior and when negativity rests only on feelings, wishes, biography, external constraints, or hidden context.
Mark uncertain when the displayed text cannot support yes or no.
Sustained Conflict is yes exactly when two immediately adjacent entries are both yes; no or uncertain breaks a run.

entries.journal_entry:
Uncle Faisal brought up university again at Friday lunch. Sat there smiling while baba nodded along like I haven't been paying my own phone bill since I was nineteen. Nobody asks how many drivers I'm running now or that I closed a deal with two new restaurants this week.

Nudge: "Sounds like you wanted them to see something different."

Response: I wanted them to see that I'm building something. That I'm not just some kid running errands on a scooter. But in their heads the only version of success they understand has a degree attached to it.

One of my riders, Sami, asked if he could take Friday off for a family thing. I said yes but it threw the whole schedule off and I ended up doing his route myself. Kept thinking about whether being the one who fixes everything is sustainable or if I just can't stand not being in the middle of it.

Nudge: "What would it feel like to let someone else handle it?"

Met with the owner of Shawarma House on Mecca Street about getting their lunch deliveries. He kept talking to his manager instead of me, asking him questions like I was some intern. I'm the one who handles the logistics, I'm the one signing the agreement, but apparently I don't look old enough to be taken seriously.

Got the deal in the end because my prices were better than the other guy's. But the whole meeting left a bad taste. I keep replaying the moments where he looked past me. I should be used to it by now—people always underestimate the young one in the room—but it still gets under my skin every time.

Nudge: "What would've made it feel like a real win?"

Response: If he'd looked me in the eye and talked to me directly. If he'd treated the meeting like I was the one making decisions, not some errand boy. The money is good but I need people to understand who they're dealing with.

Good day actually. Had all three scooters running, no breakdowns, deliveries going smooth. Sami and Yousef both hit their targets and I told them I'd add a small bonus this week. Felt good telling them that, like I'm actually running something real. Standing in the kitchen tracking the routes on my phone and for a second it felt like I had a grip on things.

My friend Khaled called to see if I wanted to come play football but I told him I had to stay on the routes. Truth is the routes were fine, I just didn't want to step away. When things are going well I want to be right there watching it happen. Maybe that's weird but I don't trust good days to last if I'm not paying attention.

Second entry today because something's been sitting with me since this morning. Baba asked if I could help in the shop tomorrow because his back is bad again. I said fine but inside I was annoyed. It's his shop, his thing—he never asks my opinion on how to run it, never treats me like I might actually know something about business.

But then I thought about it more and realized part of the annoyance is that when I'm in his shop I'm just his son moving boxes. Not the guy managing a fleet. I shrink in that space and I hate it. I don't think he does it on purpose. He just doesn't see me the way I need to be seen.

Nudge: "What would it take to feel like yourself there?"

Yousef quit today. Said he got a warehouse job that pays steady. I told him good luck but when he left I punched the wall in my room. Losing a rider means losing a route means losing income.

Nudge: "Was the anger about the lost route, or something else?"

Response: Both. He was my first hire. I trained him, gave him a shot when nobody else would. And he just walks away like it meant nothing. Like what I built wasn't worth staying for.

Spent the morning interviewing two guys to replace Yousef. One of them, Issa, seemed okay but kept asking about hours and breaks before even asking what the job was. The other one, Nader, was hungrier—reminded me of myself when I started. I told him he could do a trial run tomorrow. Already planning his routes in my head.

I notice I always pick the one who reminds me of me. The one who wants it. Khaled says I should pick the reliable one but reliable is boring. I want someone who's going to hustle because they want to prove something, not just clock in and clock out. Maybe that's a mistake. Yousef was reliable and he still left. At least with the hungry ones you know what drives them.

Nudge: "Has picking the hungry one worked out before?"

Response: Honestly, it's fifty-fifty. Some of them burn bright and then flame out. But the ones who stay—they get it, they understand what we're building. Sami was like that. He's still here. I'd rather have one Sami than three guys who don't care.

Nader didn't show up for his trial. Didn't call, didn't text. I covered his entire route myself, thirteen deliveries in the rain. My hands are still shaking from the cold.

Nudge: "You chose him because he reminded you of yourself."

Response: Yeah. And I was wrong. Or maybe he's exactly like me and decided he didn't want to answer to someone else. I don't know which is worse.

Can't sleep so I'm writing again. Today wore me out but it's not just the deliveries. I keep thinking about this pattern where I put myself at the center of everything and then get angry when people don't orbit around me the way I want. Yousef left because the warehouse was better for him. Nader didn't show because he had his own reasons. Neither of them owed me anything beyond the work.

But it doesn't feel that way. It feels like when people leave or let me down, they're saying what I'm building doesn't matter. And if what I'm building doesn't matter then what am I? Just some guy with three scooters and a spreadsheet. My mother keeps telling me I work too hard and I should find a girl and settle down like my cousin Ahmad. Ahmad works at a bank. Ahmad has a car. But Ahmad also asks his father permission before he goes anywhere and I'd rather eat glass than live like that.

I think the reason I can't let go of anything—the routes, the negotiations, even the small stuff—is because the moment I'm not in charge of something, I feel like I'm nobody. That's not a comfortable thing to write down. I don't know what to do with it. But Yousef quitting and Nader ghosting in the same week made something crack open and I'm just sitting here looking at it.

Nudge: "What are you seeing now that cracked open?"

Response: That maybe I've been so focused on people respecting me that I forgot to ask if I respect myself without all of this. Like if the scooters disappeared tomorrow and I was just Tariq, not Tariq who runs a fleet—would that be enough? I don't think I know the answer yet.

Hired Issa after all—the "reliable" one Khaled told me about. He did his first full week and showed up every day on time. Not flashy, doesn't try to impress me, just does the work. I'm not used to that being enough but I'm trying to let it be.

Had dinner at home tonight and baba asked about the business for the first time without me bringing it up. He asked how many restaurants I work with now and when I said eleven he went quiet for a second and then said "ya'ni, that's more than I thought." It wasn't a celebration or anything. But he looked at me differently, even for a moment, and I carried that home with me like it was something fragile.

I keep coming back to what I wrote last week about needing to be in charge to feel like somebody. I don't think that feeling goes away overnight. But today Issa handled the west Amman routes without me checking in once, and baba acknowledged my work without me having to perform it for him. Two small things. Neither of them was about me being in control. Both of them felt good in a way I wasn't expecting. I'm not saying I've figured anything out. But maybe not everything has to go through me for it to count.

Spent the morning reviewing proposals from two potential investors. One offers more capital but wants a board seat and quarterly reviews. The other is smaller but completely hands-off. Everyone keeps telling me the bigger check is the obvious choice. But I keep thinking about three years ago when I walked away from a salary that would make most people's eyes water, all because I couldn't stand someone else dictating my calendar.

Maybe I'm being naive. Maybe smart founders take the bigger check and learn to play the game. But every time I imagine sitting in those quarterly reviews, justifying my decisions to people who've never built anything themselves, something in my chest tightens.

Nudge: "What does that tightening in your chest usually signal for you?"

Response: It's the same feeling I had in my last corporate job. Before I quit. Like I'm slowly being boxed in. When I was younger I thought I'd outgrow it, learn to compromise. But if anything it's gotten stronger. I think that feeling is my compass now—when it shows up, I know I'm about to betray something important about how I want to live.
Took the smaller investment. Signed papers yesterday. Celebrated with Layla at that new Persian restaurant near the Marina. Felt right.

My mother called from Tehran today. She wanted to know when I'm getting married, when I'm having children, when I'm going to "settle down and stop playing." I'm almost forty. I've heard these questions my whole adult life. Usually I brush them off. Today it got under my skin more than usual.

I think it's because part of me wonders if she has a point. Not about marriage specifically—I'll figure that out when I figure it out. But about what I'm building toward. I work hard, yes, but I also make sure to enjoy the fruits of that work. Weekends in Oman. Good wine. A nice apartment. She sees all of that as frivolous, as avoiding responsibility. In her world, you sacrifice everything for family, for the future, for some distant reward.

But I've watched people sacrifice their entire lives for that distant reward and then die before they get there. Or they get there and realize they forgot how to enjoy anything. I don't want that. I refuse that. And yet—today, after we hung up, I sat on my balcony for an hour just staring at the water, wondering if my version of a good life will leave me alone at sixty with nothing but nice memories.

Nudge: "It sounds like you're weighing two different visions of what a meaningful life looks like. Which one feels more true to who you are right now?"

Response: The one where I'm present. Where I'm actually living my days instead of deferring them. I've tried the other way—grinding through miserable weeks for some hypothetical payoff. It made me sick, literally. Migraines, insomnia. My body rejected it. So I know what's true for me. I just wish knowing made it easier to hear my mother's disappointment.

Layla and I got into it last night. She thinks I work too much. I think that's rich coming from someone who doesn't understand what it takes to run your own thing. When you're employed, you clock out and your problems stay at the office. When it's YOUR company, it follows you everywhere.

But then—I don't know. She made this comment about how I used to be more fun. More spontaneous. And it stung because she's not wrong. The last few months I've been grinding harder than usual, trying to prove to myself that taking the smaller check was the right call. Trying to show I can grow this thing without giving up control.

I hate that I'm becoming the person I swore I wouldn't become. The one who's always "busy."

Nudge: "You mentioned you 'hate becoming the person you swore you wouldn't become.' What would that person look like if you fully became them?"

Response: I don't want to think about that right now. I just needed to get this out. I know what I need to do—I need to take her somewhere nice for the holidays and actually be present. That's the plan.

We went to Muscat for three days. Left my laptop at home—Layla's one condition. First day I was restless, kept reaching for my phone. By the third day I had forgotten what I was even anxious about.

We hiked to this natural pool in the mountains. Cold water, complete silence except for birds. I floated on my back and watched clouds move and thought: this is why I built my own thing. Not for the money, not for the status. For moments like this, whenever I want them. The ability to disappear for three days without asking permission.

I need to protect this. The business should serve the life, not the other way around.

Back from holiday. January pipeline already overwhelming. Three new client requests, two urgent. I said yes to all of them because I'm still in growth mode. Already regretting it.

Nudge: "When you say you're already regretting it, what specifically is showing up for you?"

Response: The familiar dread. Knowing the next two months will be meetings and deadlines and no room to breathe. Knowing I just had a beautiful reset and I'm already overcommitting. It's a pattern. I recognize it. I just don't know how to break it when saying no feels like leaving money on the table.

My old colleague Faisal reached out. He's now a VP at a big consulting firm, wanted to know if I'd consider coming back to corporate. "Senior role, name your salary, you'd have a team of twenty."

I said no, obviously. But then he made this comment—"You're still doing the solo thing? I thought that was a phase." And I felt this flash of anger. Like I needed to justify myself. Started listing my clients, my revenue, my growth numbers. Trying to prove I wasn't some hobbyist.

Later I wondered why I cared so much. Why his opinion mattered. I left that world for a reason. I don't owe anyone an explanation.

Nudge: "What do you think Faisal's comment touched on that made you want to prove yourself?"

Response: Maybe the fear that I'm fooling myself. That everyone else sees something I don't—that I gave up a real career to play entrepreneur, and one day it'll all fall apart and I'll have nothing to show for it. I know that's not rational. My business is healthy. But there's this voice that sounds a lot like my father, saying I'm not serious enough. That real men build empires, not lifestyles. I thought I'd silenced that voice. Apparently not.

Funny thing happened today. One of my clients—a resort chain—asked if I'd relocate to Bahrain for six months to oversee an opening. Great money, interesting project. My immediate reaction was no. Not because of the work, but because I imagined six months without my Thursday dinners at that Lebanese place, without my morning runs along the creek, without the routines that make my life here feel like mine.

Then I caught myself and thought—wait, am I becoming rigid? I built this life precisely so I could be flexible. So I could say yes to adventures. But now I'm protecting routines like they're sacred.

Or maybe routines are sacred. Maybe I've earned the right to build a life I don't want to escape from. Maybe flexibility isn't about saying yes to every opportunity but about having the freedom to say no.

I don't know. I told them I'd think about it over the weekend.

Nudge: "You mentioned earning 'the right to build a life you don't want to escape from.' What did it cost you to earn that right?"

Turned down the Bahrain project. Explained that I could consult remotely but wouldn't relocate. They accepted, which surprised me. I expected them to walk away. Maybe I've been underestimating my leverage. Maybe I've been saying yes to things out of scarcity when I could afford to negotiate.

Layla was relieved. She said, "You're finally acting like someone who knows what he wants." I think she's right. For years I was running from things—from my old career, from expectations, from boredom. Now I'm starting to run toward something specific. A life that actually fits. It's a subtle shift but it feels significant.

We're planning another trip for March. Somewhere new. The reward for good work isn't more work—it's more life.

Morning handoff and Dr. Salwa walked right over my patient update — just started talking to the resident as if I was not standing there midsentence. I had been going through the post-op notes for the patient in bed four, the one with the complicated drain placement, and she cut in with her own assessment before I even finished. The resident looked at me for a second like he was not sure who to listen to, then turned back to her. That was it.

I have been doing handoffs on this floor since before Salwa finished her residency. I know which details the night team needs flagged and which ones can wait. But standing there with my clipboard half-raised, watching everyone pivot to her as if my report was background noise — something about it stayed with me the whole shift. I did not say anything. Just waited until she finished and picked up where I left off like nothing happened.

Nudge: "What kept you from interrupting her back?"

Still thinking about the supply request from last week. I put in an order for the new wound-care kits on Tuesday — the ones the infection control committee recommended after the audit. I filled out the procurement form, got the unit manager to countersign, followed up with central supply the next morning. Everything by the book. And then nothing. Three days, no confirmation, no rejection, just silence.

I called down to procurement this afternoon and the clerk told me the order was "pending review" by the hospital purchasing coordinator. She could not tell me when it would move forward. Said I should "check back in a few days." So I am sitting here with patients who need updated dressings and I cannot do anything except wait for someone in an office I have never visited to decide whether my clinical judgment warrants a purchase order.

This is the part of the job that nobody sees. I can manage a twelve-bed unit, keep families informed, catch medication errors before they reach the patient, but I cannot get wound-care supplies without begging a clerk to push a form through. The dependency is what gets to me. I did everything right and I still have no control over the outcome. If it were up to me, charge nurses would have direct procurement authority for anything under five hundred dinars. But it is not up to me.

Nudge: "What does that waiting feel like?"

Response: Like I am back to being a junior nurse asking permission to change a dressing. Twenty-eight years and I still have to stand in line behind someone who has never set foot on a ward. It is not about the kits really. It is about the fact that the hospital has structured things so that someone with my experience has no more say than a fresh graduate when it comes to getting what my patients need. That is the part that sits in my chest.

Normal Friday. Did a double check on all the discharge paperwork before the weekend rush, caught a missing signature on one of the forms that would have bounced back from records. The new nurse, Hana, asked me to walk her through the IV pump calibration again. I do not mind showing people things but she has asked three times now and at some point you have to just do it yourself.

Had mansaf at my sister's house in the evening. My brother-in-law was going on about his new car, the leather seats, the financing deal he negotiated. I ate, nodded, helped clear the table. It was fine. Quiet drive home, listened to the news. Nothing remarkable about any of it.

The new infection control policy came down from administration today. They want us to change the way we do central line dressings — different kit, different sequence, different documentation. I have been doing central line care for over two decades. I wrote the current protocol for this unit. And the committee that rewrote it did not consult a single bedside nurse. Not one.

I brought it up in the staff meeting. Said the current approach has kept our infection rates below the hospital average for three years running. Dr. Fares — on staff for maybe eighteen months — pushed back hard, said the new evidence was clear and we needed to "get on board." The unit manager sided with him, of course, he has the letters after his name. So we are switching. I said fine. But I know what works on this floor. I gave in because fighting it further would have made me look like the old guard resisting change, which is exactly the label they want to stick on me.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you had kept pushing?"

Response: They would have overruled me anyway and I would have looked like I cannot adapt. At least this way I went along on my own terms. If the infection rates go up in the next quarter, the numbers will speak for themselves. That is my leverage. Not the meeting room.

Second entry today because this is still eating at me. After the meeting, the unit manager pulled me aside and asked if I could "mentor" two of the newer nurses on the updated dressing protocol. As if I need to be managed into compliance. As if twenty-some years of keeping this floor running are not proof enough that I know how to implement a procedure.

She said it casually, like it was a compliment — "you're the most experienced, they'll learn best from you." But I heard what she was really saying. She was checking whether I would fall in line. Whether I would carry the new policy to the team or quietly undermine it. The fact that she felt the need to have that conversation at all tells me something about where I stand. My track record should speak for itself. It used to.

Nudge: "Could it have just been a compliment?"

Response: Maybe. But you learn to read the room after this many years. When someone has already decided to override your judgment, asking you to teach their decision to others is not a compliment — it is containment. And the worst part is I will do it. I will teach Hana and the others exactly how the new protocol works, and I will do it well, because that is what I do. But I will know what it cost me to smile through it.

Long shift, short-staffed again. Covered the floor mostly solo after lunch when Rami called in sick. Made it through, patients are stable, paperwork is done. Going to sit on the balcony with tea and not think about the hospital for a few hours.

Neighborhood council meeting tonight and Abu Fadi talked over me three times while I was presenting the building maintenance budget. Three times. Everyone just sat there. I chair this council and the man cannot let me finish a sentence about a pipe repair without jumping in with his own opinion about which plumber to call.

Nudge: "You're the chair — what stopped you from shutting that down?"

Response: Because if I come down hard on him he will rally the older residents against me. Abu Fadi has lived in this building since before I moved in. He thinks seniority in the building means seniority on the council. It does not, but half the neighbors see it his way. So I let him talk and then I steer the vote. It works but it should not be necessary.

Had to request time off for my daughter's university orientation next month and the process was humiliating. I submitted the form three weeks in advance, well within policy. The scheduling coordinator — a woman who has been in the role for maybe a year — told me she would "try to accommodate" the request but could not guarantee it because of staffing levels. Try to accommodate. As if I am asking for a favor and not exercising a benefit I have earned.

I asked her to check the seniority list. I have more years on this unit than anyone currently on staff. My requests should be prioritized accordingly. She said the new scheduling system does not weight seniority the way the old one did, that it is "needs-based" now. So a nurse with two years of experience and no dependents has the same standing as me when it comes to getting a day off. I asked to speak with the nursing director and was told she would "follow up by email."

I am not someone who makes a scene. I thanked the coordinator, walked out, went back to my patients. But I spent the rest of the shift turning it over. Thirty years I have given this hospital. I have trained half the people on this floor. And the institution has decided that none of that earns me the simple courtesy of knowing I can attend my daughter's orientation. The system does not recognize what it owes the people who built it.

Nudge: "What would it look like if they did recognize it?"

Response: It would look like my request being approved without a conversation. It would look like the scheduling coordinator checking my file, seeing twenty-eight years, and saying "done." Not a negotiation. Not a follow-up email. Just the respect that comes from having given most of your working life to one place. That is all I am asking for. It is not complicated.

Took my mother to her cardiology appointment this morning before my shift. She is doing better — the new medication seems to have stabilized her blood pressure. The cardiologist recognized me from the hospital, called me "brother Tariq" which made my mother smile. She told him I was the best nurse in Jordan, which is not true but I did not correct her.

Stopped at Abu Mahmoud's bakery on the way back for knafeh. My mother insisted on paying even though I had my wallet out. She does that — never lets me cover anything when we are together, says a mother's duty does not expire. I let her. We ate in the car in the hospital parking lot and she told me about a cousin's wedding in Irbid next month. Simple morning. Good morning.

Quality improvement committee met today and proposed a "shared leadership" model for the nursing units. The idea is to rotate the charge nurse role every quarter so more people "develop leadership skills." They presented it like it was progressive. Like it was about growth. What it actually is, is a way to flatten the hierarchy so that no one person has enough standing to push back on administrative decisions.

I said my piece. Told them continuity matters, that patients benefit from a consistent leader who knows every case on the floor, that rotating the role creates gaps. Dr. Fares jumped in again — this seems to be his hobby now — and talked about "distributed expertise" and "empowering the whole team." The committee chair nodded along. I could feel the room shifting. I said I would review the proposal and provide written feedback, which is my way of not surrendering while buying time. But I know how this goes. They have already decided. They want my title without wanting to say they are taking it.

Nudge: "What happens to you if this goes through?"

Made a proper dinner tonight for the first time in weeks. Not reheated soup or bread and cheese at the desk, but an actual meal — roasted some chicken thighs with lemon and potatoes, opened a half-decent bottle of white wine. Sat at the table instead of the sofa. Used a real plate.

It was good. It was genuinely good. The kitchen smelled like something other than coffee for once. And then about halfway through eating I started doing the mental arithmetic — the two hours I spent cooking and shopping could have been two hours in the library, or two hours on the lit review chapter that's already a week behind. I kept eating but the food tasted different after that. Like I was doing something I needed to justify rather than just having dinner.

Nudge: "What started the counting?"

Response: Nothing specific. It just arrives. One minute I'm eating, the next I'm calculating. I think it's worse when the food is actually good — if it were just toast I wouldn't feel like I'd spent something.

Supervisor cancelled our meeting so I had an unexpected free hour. Walked to the Grote Markt and sat on a bench with a stroopwafel and watched people for a while. Pigeons, tourists, an old man feeding ducks. Didn't think about the thesis once. Small thing but I feel better than I have all week.

Nudge: "What kept the thesis out of your head?"

Barely slept last night. Got up at five because I couldn't stop thinking about the methods chapter, worked until my eyes burned, then sat in the department seminar all morning where a twenty-six-year-old presented her near-complete dissertation like it was nothing. She's been at this for three years. I've been at it for four and I'm still restructuring my framework.

After the seminar I was so tired I could feel it in my bones. The kind where you know a nap or even just lying down for twenty minutes would reset everything. My flat is a ten-minute walk. But I didn't go. I sat in the department lounge instead, drank bad coffee, and opened my laptop. Because what would it look like — the oldest person in the programme, leaving at one in the afternoon to go take a nap? Nobody would have said anything. I know that. But I would have known.

There's a version of me that would have just gone home, slept for an hour, come back sharper. That version seems like a much more sensible person. Instead I stared at a paragraph for three hours without changing a word, drove home at six feeling worse than when I started, and ate cereal standing up. I don't know who I think is keeping score but I keep playing as though someone is.

Same day, later. Couldn't sleep again so I put on a record — Bill Evans, the Village Vanguard sessions — and just sat in the dark for a bit. Didn't try to make it mean anything. The music was good. I'm tired. That's all there is to report tonight.

Nudge: "Was that sitting or giving up?"

Response: Does it matter? I was sitting. The music was on. I'm not going to overanalyse listening to a record at midnight.

Took the train to Delft today for an archive visit. The reading room had those tall windows and good light. Spent three hours with primary sources and it was the kind of work that doesn't feel like work. Bought a slice of appelgebak at a cafe by the canal on the way back. Ate it slowly. No guilt arithmetic this time.

Nudge: "What was different about today?"

Response: I think it's because the cake came after the work. The order matters to me more than I'd like to admit. If I'd had the cake first, I would have spent the archive hours thinking about whether I deserved it. This way it felt earned. Which is a depressing way to relate to a piece of cake, when I write it out.

Back from Delft, sitting at home. My neighbour Pieter invited me for a drink earlier — just a beer on his terrace, nothing complicated. And I wanted to go. It sounded genuinely nice. But the whole time I was there I couldn't stop thinking about the fact that I have a supervisor meeting on Monday and the chapter draft is still rough. Pieter was telling me about his boat, the repairs he's doing, and I was nodding but running paragraphs through my head.

He noticed, I think. Asked if everything was alright. I said I was just tired from the trip. Which was true but not the whole truth. The whole truth is that I don't know how to enjoy a beer on a Thursday evening without a running tally in the background telling me what it's costing.

Spent most of Saturday at the university library. Productive enough — reorganised my notes on the Bijlmermeer case study and drafted two pages that might actually survive the next revision. Not exciting but solid work.

Walked home through the park. The leaves are mostly gone now. Passed a man walking three dogs who looked happier than anyone I've seen in the department all month. Made a pasta with anchovies and garlic for dinner, the one I learned from an Italian colleague years ago. Watched half a documentary about post-war Dutch architecture that I'd been meaning to get to. Fell asleep on the sofa before it finished, woke up at two in the morning with a stiff neck.

Normal day. Nothing to report, really. Sometimes that's fine.

Nudge: "When isn't it fine?"

Today was a choice. My old colleague Hendrika, from the Rotterdam days, was in town and invited me for lunch at a place we used to go — Indonesian rijsttafel, the kind of meal that takes two hours and you leave feeling like you've been properly fed for the first time in months. I haven't seen her since I started the PhD. She's good company, sharp, funny, the sort of person who makes you remember you used to be someone other than a stressed-out student.

I didn't go. I told her I had a deadline, which is technically true in the way that there is always a deadline. What actually happened is I sat at my desk, revised three paragraphs, went to a supervision prep meeting that lasted forty minutes, and spent the rest of the afternoon reading a paper I could have read tomorrow or next week or never. The work was fine. It was useful. But it wasn't urgent, and I knew that when I made the choice.

By evening I was sitting with the familiar feeling of having picked the responsible thing and gotten nothing in return except a quiet kind of bleakness. Hendrika sent a photo of the rijsttafel with a message that said "you were missed." I looked at it for a long time. The food looked incredible. I ate a sandwich at my desk.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you'd gone?"

Response: I would have enjoyed it. That's the honest answer. I would have had a good meal with a good friend and come back to the same work in the same state it was in before. Nothing would have fallen apart. I know that. I just couldn't make myself believe it at the time.

December already. Dark by half four. Cycling home tonight the air had that cold sharpness that makes you feel awake whether you want to or not. Locked my bike, walked up the stairs, and instead of going straight to the laptop I did something I haven't done in weeks — ran a bath. Not a shower, a proper bath. Found some old bath oil under the sink that I don't even remember buying, probably left over from when Anneke was still visiting.

Lay there for maybe forty minutes. The water was too hot at first, then just right. I could hear the wind outside and the neighbours' television through the wall, some game show. My mind wandered but not to work, which surprised me. I thought about the flat I had in Rotterdam, the one with the balcony where I used to grow herbs. Thought about a holiday I took in Portugal years ago where I spent a whole day doing nothing except swimming and reading on a beach. It felt like remembering a different person.

Got out when the water went cold, put on clean clothes, made tea. Sat at the kitchen table and just drank it. The thesis will be there in the morning. Tonight, I think, this was enough. I don't say that often. I'm trying to notice when I do.

Ma called three times during the supplier meeting this afternoon. Three times. Each time I stepped out, and each time it was about the pharmacy not having her specific brand of blood pressure pills. By the third call I was short with her and I could hear her voice go small on the other end.

Drove to two pharmacies after the meeting to find the right ones. Got home and she'd already eaten dinner alone in her room. Lifen said Ma wouldn't come out. I left the pills outside her door and just sat in the car for twenty minutes in the garage not wanting to go back inside. I know she's scared about her health. I know that. But the way my chest tightened when the phone rang that third time — I don't want to be this person.

Nudge: "What kind of person do you think you were being?"

Response: The impatient kind. Anyway she was fine by morning, we had congee together.

Spent Sunday reorganizing Ma's medication schedule into a weekly pill organizer. Color-coded it, printed the times in large font. She watched me do it and kept saying I was wasting my time, that she could remember on her own. But last month she doubled up on her thyroid medication twice so clearly she can't.

There's something about the way she resists help that makes me try harder. Lifen pointed out I spent four hours on this when I had quarterly reports due. She's not wrong. But if I don't do it, who does? Yuxin is in Hong Kong. Haojie has mock exams. Lifen already handles enough. I keep circling back to this — the math doesn't work, there's only one of me, and everyone needs a piece.

Nudge: "What happens to the piece that's yours?"

Had dinner with old university friends last night. Lao Zhang just sold his logistics company, talking about retiring to Yunnan, painting landscapes. Everyone congratulated him. Someone turned to me and asked when I'd slow down too, said I looked tired. I told them I'm fine, business is growing, we're expanding into Southeast Asian markets.

On the drive home I kept replaying it. I don't look tired. Or maybe I do. But what am I supposed to do — Haojie needs tutoring fees paid, Ma's specialist appointments aren't cheap even with insurance, and Yuxin's Hong Kong rent went up again. Lao Zhang doesn't have parents to look after, his kids are already working. Easy to paint landscapes when nobody's depending on you.

It's not that I'm jealous. I chose this. When Ba died and Ma needed somewhere to go, I didn't hesitate. When Lifen suggested Yuxin should study locally to save money, I said no, let her go to Hong Kong, it's better for her career. These were my decisions and I'd make them again. I just wish people would stop looking at me like I'm some kind of cautionary tale when I'm doing exactly what I should be doing.

Nudge: "Who are you convincing — them or yourself?"

Response: Them. I know what I'm doing and why. The difference between me and Lao Zhang isn't that he's free and I'm stuck. He just doesn't have anyone who needs him the way my family needs me. That's not a burden, that's the whole point.
Haojie asked me to quiz him on chemistry formulas after dinner. Sat at the dining table for an hour while Ma peeled fruit for us. Nobody needed anything from me except to be there.

Client dinner ran until 11pm. Mr. Tanaka from Osaka — important account, been courting him for months. He wanted to do KTV after dinner, brought two of his managers. Good business practice to go along, build the relationship. So I went.

Lifen had texted at 9pm that Ma had a dizzy spell. Not serious, she said, blood pressure spiked a bit but came back down. She handled it, took Ma's readings, made her lie down. Everything was managed. I read the text between songs and replied "OK take care of her, back late." Lifen didn't text again.

Got home past 1am. House was dark. Checked on Ma — sleeping. Checked on Lifen — also sleeping, or pretending to. Stood in the hallway for a while. The deal with Tanaka matters, the revenue would cover Yuxin's next semester easily. But there's this feeling like I filed something in the wrong drawer and now I can't find it.

Nudge: "What drawer should it have gone in?"

Response: I should have called. Not texted "OK." Even five minutes, just to hear Ma's voice, ask Lifen if she needed me to come back. I chose convenient over present and I can feel it sitting in my stomach.

Tanaka confirmed the contract this morning so at least the evening wasn't wasted. Lifen said Ma is fine. Everyone's fine.

Nudge: "You keep saying fine — you believe that?"

Response: Honestly, no. Ma was fine medically, sure. But Lifen's been quieter than usual and I think she's keeping score of these nights. Not in a petty way. She just notices when I choose work over home and I think last night added to whatever tally she keeps.

Yuxin called today, long call, almost forty minutes. She's been struggling with a group project — three teammates not pulling their weight, and she's doing most of the analysis herself. She was venting and halfway through she stopped and said "Ba, am I being unreasonable? Maybe I should just do it all and not make a fuss."

And I heard myself in her. That exact calculation — just absorb it, carry it, don't rock anything, because the outcome matters more than being right. I told her no, she should talk to her teammates, set expectations. That she doesn't have to shoulder everything alone. The words came out so easily when they were for her.

After we hung up I sat with that for a while. Lifen came in, saw my face, asked what was wrong. I said nothing, just a good talk with Yuxin. She squeezed my shoulder on her way out. These are the moments where everything makes sense — my daughter calls me when things are hard, my wife knows when I need a hand on my shoulder without being told. Whatever else is messy, this part works. This part I would burn everything down to keep.

Skipped Haojie's parent-teacher meeting because a shipment got held at customs. Lifen went instead. He didn't say anything about it but he also didn't ask me to quiz him tonight.

Nudge: "What do you think he didn't say?"

Response: That it mattered to him. That he wanted me there specifically, not just a parent. Lifen's great at these things but I told him last week I'd go this time. I broke that and acted like customs clearance is a good enough excuse. It's not. It's just the one I had.

Took Haojie for beef noodles after school today. Didn't mention yesterday. He talked about his physics teacher the whole time and it almost felt normal but not quite.

Nudge: "What was the 'not quite' part?"

Response: Maybe I'm overthinking it. He ate two bowls so he can't be that upset.

Ma's birthday this Saturday. Called Yuxin to plan — she's flying in Friday night, insisting on buying the cake from that specific bakery in Futian that Ma likes. Haojie is making a card, which he hasn't done since he was maybe twelve. Lifen already ordered Ma's favorite fish from the wet market, the one she has to get there at 6am for.

None of this was coordinated. I didn't send a family group chat saying "plan birthday." Yuxin just asked when she should arrive. Haojie just started drawing without being told. Lifen just ordered the fish. They all moved toward the same thing separately because they know it matters.

I don't know how to say this without sounding sentimental but this is what I've been building. Not the company, not the contracts with Tanaka. This — the part where everyone in my family independently decides to show up for each other. Ma probably won't even like the cake that much. She'll say it's too sweet. But she'll save the card and put it next to the ones from when Yuxin was small, and that will be the whole thing right there.

Factory audit next week, Lifen handling payroll errors again, Ma wants me to drive her to see Auntie Fang on Saturday. Haojie left his physics textbook at school. Everyone orbiting and I'm the gravity.

Nudge: "Gravity holds things together or pulls you down?"

Response: Both. Same force. On good days it's the first thing, on tired days it's the second. Today I'm somewhere in between — Auntie Fang's been lonely since her husband passed so I'll drive Ma there. Not because I have to. Because Ma will worry about Auntie Fang all week if she doesn't visit and then I'll worry about Ma.

The coordinadora pulled me aside after class and told me my reading circle format doesn't align with the ministry's new standardized framework. She wants me to switch to the workbook model by next week. I said I'd look into it but I'm not changing what's working.

Nudge: "What happens when next week comes and you haven't switched?"

People keep acting like I'm being difficult but I sat through two years of watching kids at the private school fill out comprehension worksheets with dead eyes. My students here actually talk about the books we read. They argue about characters. Last week Sofia, who barely spoke in September, stood up and defended her interpretation of the ending of the story to the whole class. That doesn't come from a workbook.

I talked to the coordinadora again today and told her straight up that I'd document my students' progress however she needs but I won't gut the program to fit a checkbox. She didn't love it. But she didn't say no either. My mom thinks I should just comply and keep my head down, which is rich coming from someone who argued with every teacher I ever had.

Nudge: "Your mom told you to comply. How did that land?"

Response: It stung more than it should have. She's the reason I push back on things in the first place.

Took Camilo through practice problems for the ICFES all Saturday morning. He's getting better at the reading comprehension sections, still shaky on the quantitative reasoning but that's not really my wheelhouse. I set him up with a YouTube channel that breaks it down step by step and told him to message me if he gets stuck. His mom, my tia Elena, called from Medellin to thank me, which was nice.

Went to abuela's in the afternoon. She'd made sancocho even though it was just the two of us, enough for ten people as usual. We watched a novela together and I fixed the latch on her back door that's been loose for months. She seems okay. A little quieter than normal maybe, but she always gets like that around the anniversary of abuelo's passing.

Long day. Parent meetings ran until six and then I stayed to reorganize the classroom library because nobody else was going to. Camilo called with a question about a practice test but I was too fried to be useful so I told him we'd go over it this weekend.

Nudge: "You couldn't help Camilo tonight. Does that bother you?"

Response: Yeah, a little. He doesn't ask unless he's actually stuck.

Got an email from the district office about a new mandate requiring all language arts teachers to submit weekly lesson plans using their template. The template is basically a fill-in-the-blank thing with pre-set learning objectives and approved activities. I forwarded it to my colleague Patricia and she just shrugged and said she'd start using it. I get it, she has two kids and no energy to fight bureaucracy. But I can't just do that.

The thing is, I know how this looks. I know people see me as the one who always has to do things her own way, the difficult teacher, the one who won't just go along. And maybe there's some truth in it. But I've also seen what happens when you hand over every decision about your classroom to someone who's never met your students. The kids in Ciudad Bolivar don't need another generic worksheet. They need someone paying attention to what actually reaches them.

I'm going to keep using my own plans and submit the template version as a formality. I've done this before. It's more work but I'd rather do double the planning than teach something I don't believe in.

Nudge: "You said maybe there's truth in being the difficult one. What's that like?"

Response: Lonely sometimes. Patricia used to push back with me but she stopped. I don't blame her but I notice it.

Second time writing today because I can't stop thinking about something that happened after school. One of my students, Diego, waited around after everyone left and asked me if I could help him write a letter to his dad who's working in Chile. He said his mom doesn't have time and he doesn't know how to start. So we sat there for forty minutes and I helped him figure out what he wanted to say. He wanted to tell his dad about a soccer game he played in and about a dog that follows him home from school every day.

It was one of those moments where you remember why you're doing this. Not the curriculum fights or the template emails or the district mandates. Just a kid who needed someone to sit with him and say yeah, that's worth writing down, your dad would want to hear about the dog.

I keep thinking about how many kids like Diego just need someone to stop and listen for half an hour. And then I think about how stretched thin I already am and how I told Camilo I'd help him this weekend and I promised abuela I'd come by Sunday and I still haven't finished planning next week's classes because I was busy making the fake template version for the district. There's only so much of me to go around.

Spent the morning with Camilo going through the quantitative reasoning section. He's definitely improving. We worked through the problems he'd flagged and he got about seventy percent right on his own before I stepped in. Told him that's real progress and he actually smiled, which he doesn't do much these days since he's so stressed about the exam. His mom is counting on him getting into a public university because they can't afford private tuition, so the pressure is real.

After that I went to abuela's. She was in better spirits today. She'd been to the market and bought ingredients for arepas de choclo, so we made them together. She told me stories about when she and abuelo first came to Bogota from the campo and how they used to make arepas on a clay stove. I've heard the stories before but I don't mind. She seems to need to tell them.

On the bus home I sketched out a rough plan for next week's reading unit. I'm going to have the kids write letters, inspired by Diego. They'll pick someone they want to tell something to and we'll work on voice and structure through that. It fits the ministry objectives if you squint at it the right way, and it's actually something the kids will care about.

Nudge: "Diego's letter became a whole unit. Does that happen a lot?"

Response: More than I realize probably. The best ideas come from paying attention to what the kids actually need, not from a planning document.

Tia Elena called tonight and said Camilo bombed his practice test today, the one right after our session. She wasn't blaming me but it felt like it. I keep wondering if I'm actually helping him or just making myself feel useful.

Nudge: "Making yourself feel useful — what made you put it that way?"

Response: I don't know, I'm just tired. He'll be fine, he's smart enough.

My students turned in their essays on Taha Hussein today. Most of them are struggling with the language, but Layla wrote something that made me stop grading. She said his blindness made him see more clearly, that losing one sense opened the others. I sat with that for a while after school, drinking mint tea on my balcony as the call to prayer drifted up from the neighborhood mosque.

I think I understand what she meant. When I stopped chasing the next promotion, the next credential, everything else came into sharper focus. The weight of the teacup in my hands. The specific shade of orange the sunset throws on the building across the street. These aren't consolation prizes for not advancing. They're the whole point.

My sister called later and asked if I'm still "just teaching" the same classes. I said yes. She doesn't understand why I'm not bored. I didn't try to explain that I'm not looking for excitement from my job. That's not what jobs are for.

The school administration announced today that we're switching to a new standardized testing system. Six meetings this month. Saturday workshops through January. Mandatory curriculum alignment sessions.

I felt my stomach drop when I read the email. Not because the work is hard. Because I know exactly what this means. No more lingering over coffee in the morning because I'll need to leave earlier for meetings. No more Thursday evenings at the souk because Saturdays will be consumed by training. No more space in my day for the small things that make it bearable.

My colleague Tariq seems energized by it. He was talking about "opportunities for growth" and "showcasing our department." I just nodded. I wanted to ask him when he last sat somewhere doing nothing and felt content. When he last ate a meal slowly enough to taste it.

I know this sounds dramatic. It's just meetings. It's just one semester. But that's how it always starts. First it's one semester, then it's a "new normal," and before you know it you've forgotten what it felt like to have time that belongs to you.

Mama would say I should be grateful to have a job at all. She's right, probably. But gratitude and resentment can live in the same body.

Nudge: "What would you lose if you pushed back?"

Response: Everything and nothing. I'd lose standing with the administration. I'd be labeled as difficult, uncooperative. Eventually it would affect my references if I ever wanted to move.

But what I'd really lose is the illusion that I can have it both ways. That I can care about my peace and also be a "team player" in a system that treats peace as laziness. Right now I'm invisible, which means I'm safe. If I push back, I become visible in the wrong way.

The truth is I probably won't say anything. I'll go to the meetings and protect what I can and hope it blows over. I'm not proud of this. But I also know that martyrdom doesn't taste good.

Second Saturday workshop done. Made foul for breakfast beforehand. Good olive oil makes a difference, even at 6am. Workshop was tedious but I sat near the window where the light was warm. Found a new bakery on the way home. Their knafeh has the right amount of orange blossom.

Sometimes survival is about noticing what's still good.

Christmas isn't our holiday but the city has lights everywhere and I love them anyway. Walking home tonight the whole street was glowing, gold and white, and I stopped to just look. A shopkeeper came out to smoke and we stood there together not saying anything, just watching. He offered me a sesame cookie from a paper bag. Still warm.

This is what I mean when I say the good things aren't complicated. Lights on a cold street. A cookie from a stranger. The permission to pause.

My students are gone for break and I have two weeks with nothing required of me. I've planned nothing. That's the plan. Maybe I'll sleep late and read novels and take long baths and visit my aunt who makes the best maqluba. Or maybe I'll do something else entirely. The point is I don't know yet, and not knowing feels like freedom.

Nudge: "What does that freedom feel like?"

Response: Like finally exhaling. Like my body relaxes in ways I didn't know it was tense. When there's nothing scheduled, time moves differently. An hour feels like an hour instead of like sixty minutes I'm supposed to fill with something productive.

I think most people are so used to planned time that they've forgotten the other kind exists. They schedule their relaxation, which turns it into another task. "Self-care Sunday" as an obligation. But real rest doesn't have a hashtag. It's what happens when you stop trying to optimize anything.

Back to school. The break ended too quickly, as they always do. Woke up this morning before my alarm and lay there feeling the weight of the semester ahead.

Three more Saturday workshops scheduled. Parent conferences starting next week. A new student in my advanced class who apparently has "behavioral issues" which is usually code for "nobody took time to understand him."

I made myself the fancy coffee, the Ethiopian beans I save for hard mornings. It helped a little. But even the coffee felt rushed. I was drinking it to finish it, not to enjoy it.

This is what I'm afraid of. That the system will eventually win not by forcing me to change but by leaving me no room to be who I am. That I'll adapt and adapt until I can't remember what I was adapting away from.

Dramatic again, I know. It's just the first day talking. By next week I'll have found my rhythm. I always do.

Nudge: "Who were you before you started adapting?"

Response: Someone with more time, probably. That's the easy answer.

The harder answer is I'm not sure I was anyone different. Maybe I've always been negotiating between what I want and what's required. Even as a child I remember preferring slow afternoons to busy schedules, choosing books over activities that might have "developed my potential."

Maybe the question isn't who I was but whether there's a version of me that wouldn't have to fight for this. Someone who naturally craves what the world rewards. That person doesn't exist, though. So here I am.

Something shifted this week. Not in my circumstances, but in how I'm holding them.

I've started bringing my good tea to school. The loose-leaf oolong that I usually save for weekends. I keep it in a small tin in my desk drawer and brew it during my free period. The ritual takes five minutes that I technically don't have, but those five minutes change the texture of the entire day.

My colleague Hana noticed and asked where I got the tea. We ended up talking about her grandmother who used to make her own herb blends. Now Hana brings me a cup of her za'atar tea on Tuesdays, and I share my oolong on Thursdays. It's become a small island of niceness in the week.

I think I'd been treating my work life and my real life as separate territories, with work as enemy land where beauty couldn't survive. But that's not true. Beauty is portable. You can carry it with you if you're willing to make room for it.

The Saturday workshops are still tedious. The new testing system is still pointless. But I'm finding cracks where something good can grow.

My brother visited this weekend. He's an engineer in Dubai now, making good money, working long hours. He looks tired but won't admit it.

Over dinner he asked when I'm "going to do something" with my career. I said I'm doing something with it. I teach. He said I know what he means. Higher positions. More responsibility. A path that leads somewhere.

I told him my path leads to my apartment every evening in time to cook dinner and read before bed. He laughed like I was joking. When he realized I wasn't, he got that look. The look that says he's worried about me.

I hate that look. Like contentment is a symptom of something wrong. Like a woman my age without ambition must be hiding some failure or fear.

Maybe I am afraid. Afraid of waking up exhausted every day like he does. Afraid of forgetting what rest feels like. If that's fear, I'm not ashamed of it.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to stop explaining yourself?"

Response: Honestly? I think I'd have to stop caring what they think. Which sounds simple but isn't.

Their opinions shouldn't matter. I'm nearly forty. I pay my own bills. I've built a life that works for me. But family has this way of making you feel like a child again. My brother's concern isn't really concern, it's disappointment that I'm not living the way he thinks I should. And despite everything, part of me still wants his approval.

The exhausting thing is that he'll never understand. He measures life by achievements. I measure it by how it feels to live it. We're speaking different languages.

Maybe the answer is just to stop having the conversation. Nod and change the subject. Let him think whatever he thinks. Save my energy for people who don't need me to be different.

Made mansaf from scratch today. It took hours. The lamb, the jameed sauce, all of it. My apartment smells incredible. I ate slowly, standing at the kitchen window watching the rain.

Nobody saw it. Nobody will praise me for it. It's not an accomplishment by any measure that matters to the world. But my god, it was good. The texture, the warmth, the satisfaction of having made something beautiful for no reason except that I wanted to.

This is what I protect.

Bad week. Three students failing my class because they don't do the reading. Administration pressure to raise pass rates. A parent screamed at me over email for being "too demanding" when I wouldn't accept a late assignment that was two weeks overdue.

I came home Thursday wanting to cry. Made myself do it anyway, just sat on the couch and let it happen. Then I took a bath with the expensive salts I've been saving and drank wine that was probably too good for a weeknight.

The world tells me I should fix these problems. Work harder, communicate better, be more flexible. But some problems aren't mine to fix. The student who doesn't read won't start reading because I exhaust myself trying to convince him. The parent who yells will keep yelling regardless of my response.

What I can control is the bath and the wine and the permission to feel bad without trying to optimize my way out of it.

Nudge: "What did letting yourself cry change?"

Response: It reminded me that I'm human. That sounds simple but I forget sometimes. In teacher mode I'm supposed to be endlessly patient, endlessly resourceful. There's no room for frustration that isn't immediately converted into a lesson plan improvement.

Crying let me feel the weight of the week without doing anything about it. And then it passed. The bath wasn't medicine for the crying. The crying was already the medicine. The bath was just dessert.

I think we're taught that negative emotions are problems to solve rather than experiences to have. But sometimes you just need to be sad for a while. It doesn't mean anything is wrong. It means you're paying attention.

Valentine's Day. I'm not with anyone and haven't been for a while. Most years this is fine. Today I felt the absence more than usual.

Not loneliness exactly. More like awareness that my life lacks certain kinds of warmth that come from partnership. Shared meals across a table. Someone who knows what kind of day you had without you having to explain. Inside jokes.

I walked to the old bookshop after school, the one with the narrow aisles and the owner who always recommends obscure poets. He gave me coffee and we talked about Mahmoud Darwish for half an hour. On the way home I stopped for the good pastries, the ones with rose petals pressed into the glaze.

I think there's a version of this day that would have been sad. Lonely woman, empty apartment, commercial holiday. But that's not how it felt. It felt like choosing, again, to make my own day good. To not wait for someone else to bring me flowers when I can bring myself pastries.

Mama called to ask if I was okay. I told her I'd had a lovely day. She didn't believe me, but it was true.

I know I write a lot about small pleasures. Someone reading this might think I'm avoiding the big questions. But I think the small pleasures are the big questions, answered. What do you want from life? This. A warm cup, a good book, a body that's been cared for, time that belongs to me.

Tomorrow will be another staff meeting, another round of tedious requirements. But tonight is mine. And that's enough.

Spent the whole evening comparing fixed deposits on three different bank sites while the kids fought over the remote. Anil says we should lock in the rate before it drops again. I know he is right but I kept thinking about that weekend trip to Alibaug we never took. We had the dates picked and everything.

Nudge: "What happened to the Alibaug trip?"

Response: We pushed it. First it was school fees, then Anil's mother needed new glasses, then he said let's wait till Diwali. Diwali came and went. The dates are still saved in my calendar like some kind of joke.

My sister Meera called and asked when we are finally going on a proper holiday. I told her we went to Lonavala in June. She laughed and said "that does not count, it was one night at your cousin's farmhouse." I do not need her opinion on how we spend our time or money.

Nudge: "Did it bother you because she was wrong, or because she wasn't?"

Took Aarav and Rhea for their dental checkups. No cavities, which is a relief because the last round cost us eleven thousand rupees. Came home, made dal and rice, helped Aarav with his Hindi homework. He is struggling with the matras again. Called the tuition teacher and she can take him Tuesdays and Thursdays for two thousand a month.

Anil came back late. I had kept his food covered on the counter. He ate while scrolling his phone and went straight to bed. I watched two episodes of my show and finished the leftover gulab jamun from Sunday. The house was quiet. It should have felt peaceful but it just felt flat.

My mother-in-law brought up the gold investment scheme again at lunch. She wants us to put money into one of those jeweller savings plans, says every family needs gold as a safety net. Anil looked at me like I was supposed to agree. I smiled and said we would think about it.

Later I told him I do not want more gold sitting in a locker I forget the code to. He said it is about long-term protection, not about wearing it. Fine. But we have insurance, we have the FD, we have his provident fund. At what point do we stop saving for some imagined disaster and use the money to actually live a little? He got quiet and changed the subject.

Nudge: "What would 'living a little' look like right now?"

Response: Nothing extravagant. Maybe booking that resort in Goa I bookmarked three months ago. Getting the good cheese from the fancy store without feeling guilty. Taking an auto instead of waiting twenty minutes for the bus with two cranky kids. Small things that do not require a committee meeting.

I had a full breakdown over a cake. Not even a real breakdown, just stood in the kitchen with my hands shaking after I realized I forgot to order Rhea's birthday cake. Her party is Saturday. I had the tab open on my phone for two weeks, kept meaning to customize it, kept getting pulled away. She wanted the one shaped like a castle with purple icing and her name in gold. Now the bakery says they need five days minimum and I have three.

Called four other bakeries. One can do it but it costs almost double. I said yes without even checking with Anil because I could not bear the thought of my daughter's face when there is no castle cake.

Then I sat on the kitchen floor and cried. Not because of the cake. Because of everything the cake represents. How my days dissolve into errands and appointments and packed lunches and I cannot hold onto a single thing for myself. I used to coordinate events for two hundred guests. I used to keep spreadsheets and timelines and vendor contacts organized across six months. Now I cannot order a cake on time.

Anil found the charge on the credit card and asked about it. I told him the original bakery fell through. He said okay, no problem. He did not ask why I was upset. I do not think he noticed.

The worst part is that I know the party will be fine. Rhea will love the cake. The kids will run around the flat screaming. The mothers will sit in the living room and talk about school admissions. And I will clean up afterwards and load the dishwasher and think about the next thing. That is what bothers me. Not that it went wrong. That even when it goes right, there is nothing in it for me.

Nudge: "You said 'nothing in it for me' — what would something for you look like?"

Response: I honestly do not know anymore. That scares me more than the money stuff. At least with money I know what the number should be. But when I try to picture what would make me feel like I got something out of a day, I come up blank. I used to know. I used to have answers. When did I stop?

Rhea's party went well. She wore her purple lehenga and ate cake with both hands. I took about sixty photos. At one point she grabbed my face and said "best mummy" and I felt it go straight through me.

After everyone left and the kids were asleep, I poured myself a glass of wine I had hidden behind the pickle jars. Sat on the balcony with my feet up. Pune was noisy below me — auto horns, someone's TV, a dog barking. I just sat there drinking slowly and not thinking about anything. Forty-five minutes of nothing. It was the best part of my week and it cost me two hundred rupees and zero planning.

I need more of that. Not wine specifically. Just moments that belong to me.

Long week. Aarav had a stomach bug Monday through Wednesday so I was home with him while Rhea went to school. Spent three days washing sheets, making khichdi, and checking his temperature. He is fine now, already back to leaving Lego pieces everywhere.

Anil's company announced a restructuring. Nothing definite yet but he has been tense. Came home Thursday and said we should hold off on any big expenses for a few months, just in case. I agreed because what else would I say. We ordered in biryani that night and watched a movie together on the couch. The biryani was from the place near the station, the one that uses too much oil but tastes right. Anil fell asleep halfway through the film. I finished it alone and cleaned the containers.

Nudge: "What else would you have wanted to say?"

Sunday. Took both kids to the park in the morning. Rhea did the monkey bars for the first time without help and I clapped so hard another parent looked at me. Aarav just wanted to sit on the bench and read his comic book, which is fine, he has always been like that.

Came home and looked at my recipe blog for the first time in weeks. I have a draft post about paneer tikka that I never finished. The photos are on my phone, the notes are in a notebook somewhere. I thought about sitting down to write it but then the pressure cooker started whistling and Rhea needed help with her project and the moment passed. My phone buzzed with a notification that my blog traffic dropped forty percent this month. I closed the app.

Nudge: "What does the blog mean to you these days?"

Response: It used to feel like proof I still had a brain. That I could make something and put it out there with my name on it. Now it just feels like another thing I am failing at. One more tab I keep meaning to get back to.

Renewed our health insurance today. Bumped up the coverage to ten lakhs because Anil's colleague had a surgery that wiped out his savings. Felt right to have that cushion even though the premium went up. Also renewed Aarav's accident cover since he is starting football classes next month.

Made butter chicken for dinner using my grandmother's recipe. The one with the kasuri methi at the end. Both kids asked for seconds. Anil said it was the best I have made in months, which I will take as a compliment and not an insult. After they went to bed, I sat down and actually finished the paneer tikka blog post. Published it at eleven PM. Nothing fancy, but it is out there. Felt good to hit that button.

Spent twenty minutes at the pharmacy comparing two brands of the same medication because the cheaper one had packaging I didn't recognize. Walked out with the expensive one. Too tired to explain to myself why that matters but it does.

Nudge: "What was it about the unfamiliar packaging?"

Went to the office, reviewed next quarter's staffing projections, had lunch with Faisal at the usual place. Came home, called my mother, watched half a documentary about shipping routes before falling asleep on the couch.

My cousin Nasser wants me to co-sign on a car loan for him. He's family, and I know he means well, but his track record with money is not great. I told him I'd think about it, which is the polite version of what I actually felt, which was a hard wall going up the second he asked.

Part of me wondered if I'm being too rigid. He's working now, he's trying. But I've spent three years building a buffer between myself and the kind of financial stress my parents dealt with when I was a kid, and the thought of someone else's payments showing up on my credit report makes my chest tight. I know how that sounds.

Nudge: "That wall — protective or isolating?"

Response: Both, probably. But I'd rather be isolated than exposed.

Got asked in a team meeting if I'd volunteer for the new branch setup in Jeddah. It's a promotion track, everyone knows that. I said I'd need to review the terms first, which bought me time. Staying here is not playing small — it's just not gambling what already works.

Nudge: "What would the terms need to say?"

Meal-prepped for the whole week on Saturday so I wouldn't have to think about food during work days. Reorganized the emergency drawer too — replaced the expired first aid supplies, checked the flashlight batteries. Tomorrow feels manageable because today I made it so.

Faisal told me today he thinks I overthink things. He said it casually, like it was nothing, but it stuck. He was talking about how I checked the office supply order three times before submitting it because last quarter they sent the wrong toner and it took two weeks to sort out. He thinks that's excessive. I think that's why we didn't run out of toner.

But then I'm sitting here at night and thinking about whether double-checking an office supply order is really about the toner or about something else. Whether I do it because it's smart or because the idea of something going wrong, even something small, makes me feel like I'm standing on a ledge. And I don't know. I honestly don't. But the toner was correct this time.

Nudge: "Standing on a ledge over toner — your words."

Still thinking about what Faisal said. And it's not even the toner thing specifically, it's that he said it like it was obvious to everyone around me. Like they all see this thing I do and just tolerate it. That stings more than the actual criticism.

I called my sister after work and she said something similar once, years ago, that I hold on too tight to things. She meant relationships at the time. I don't think I hold on too tight. I think I hold on the right amount and other people let go too easily and then act surprised when things fall apart.

Nudge: "What did your sister mean back then?"

Response: She was upset I kept texting her ex to make sure he returned her stuff. She thought I was overstepping. I thought I was being thorough.

You know what, I'm not going to keep circling this. Faisal has lost his wallet twice this year. Twice. And both times I was the one who helped him cancel his cards because he didn't even know which bank to call. So maybe being careful is not the personality flaw everyone wants it to be.

I run a team of fourteen people. If I operated the way other people seem to think is normal, things would fall through constantly. The reason things don't fall through is because someone checks. Someone follows up. That someone is me and I'm tired of acting like that needs defending.

Slow day at work. We're between project cycles so mostly it was inbox cleanup and a couple of one-on-ones with junior staff. Had kabsa for lunch from the place near the office that's been there since before I started this job. The owner knows my order by now.

After work I went to the gym, did the same circuit I always do, and stopped at the grocery store on the way home. They moved the rice to a different aisle which threw me off for a second but otherwise everything was fine. Watched the news, texted my mother goodnight.

Couldn't sleep so I'm writing this. There's a rumor at work that our department might get restructured. Nothing official, just something Ahmed mentioned in passing, but now I can't stop running scenarios. What if they move me to a different team. What if the new manager doesn't operate the way I've set things up. What if they decide operations can be leaner and I'm the cut.

I have six months of expenses saved. I know that. I've checked. But the knowing doesn't quiet the thing in my chest that says six months isn't enough, that nothing is enough, that the floor could always open. I protected that buffer carefully and part of me wonders if I built it because I'm wise or because I've never really believed the ground would hold.

Nudge: "Six months saved but still not enough — where does enough start?"

Response: When I stop flinching at rumors. Haven't figured out how to save my way there yet.

Had the quarterly meeting with the collective this evening. Usual stuff -- dividing the heating bill, scheduling who gets the large press for October. I ended up mediating between Sylvain and Oriane again because they both wanted the same two-week block and neither would yield. I proposed a split arrangement, alternating days, and they both agreed. Took about forty minutes to get there.

Afterward Sylvain thanked me and said the collective would fall apart without me. Meant it as a compliment, I think. But it felt like being told my role is to hold other people's work together. Not sure when I became the person who manages everyone's access instead of using the press myself. My own print series hasn't moved since July.

Walking home I passed the new gallery on Rue de la Republique. They had a group show opening, all artists around my age. I recognised one name from school. Stood outside for a minute but didn't go in. Wasn't dressed for it, and I didn't want to make small talk about what I've been working on because the honest answer is administrative emails.

Nudge: "What would've happened if you'd walked in?"

Good day actually. Spent the morning in the studio alone before anyone else arrived. Got the underpainting done on the large canvas I've been circling around for weeks. It's a night scene, the Saone from the Passerelle footbridge, and I think I finally found the right temperature for the water. Cool greens, not the blue I kept defaulting to.

My mother called at lunch. She asked about the heating bill dispute and I gave her the short version. She said I was sensible to handle it and that managing people is a real skill. Coming from her that means something because she manages twelve employees at the firm. She doesn't say things like that lightly.

Then the afternoon was admin. Filed the collective's quarterly report for the Mairie, updated the shared calendar, responded to three emails about the open studio weekend in November. Tedious but I got through it fast. There's a rhythm to it when I'm in a decent mood. The trick is not letting it eat into the morning. Mornings are mine.

Oriane told me she submitted to the Prix Dauphine. Same one I've been thinking about for two months. She asked if I'd look over her artist statement and I said yes because what else do you say. Spent twenty minutes editing her paragraphs and they were good, really good, and I haven't even started mine.

Nudge: "What's keeping yours in the drawer?"

Response: Time, mostly. Or that's what I tell myself. The application needs ten images and a statement and I keep thinking my recent work isn't cohesive enough. Oriane's is. She has a clear thread. Mine is all over the place.

Still thinking about the Prix Dauphine thing. Went back through my portfolio folder after dinner and actually it's not as scattered as I thought. The river paintings connect. The printwork connects to the paintings if you read them as studies. I could frame it. But then I keep imagining the selection panel looking at it and seeing someone who doesn't know what she is yet. Which is worse than being rejected outright honestly.

The deadline is October 15th. That's less than three weeks. If I'm going to do it I need to start the statement this weekend, pick the images, and get decent photographs of the two canvases that are only on my phone right now. Sylvain has a good camera. I could ask him but then he'd know I'm applying and if I don't get it that's a conversation I don't want.

Oriane texted to say thanks for the edits. I said bonne chance and meant it but also felt something hard in my chest when I typed it. Not jealousy exactly. More like watching someone do the thing you keep postponing and knowing it's your own fault.

Third time opening this today. It's late and I should sleep but my mind won't stop cycling. I printed Oriane's statement and mine side by side -- well, hers and the half-page of notes I have. Hers reads like someone who knows exactly who she is as an artist. Mine reads like a studio coordinator who also paints.

I'm going to set the alarm for six and work on the statement before anyone else gets to the studio. That's the plan. Whether I actually get up is another question but writing it down makes it feel slightly more real.
