Got up at six like I said. Sat at the kitchen table with coffee and opened the statement document. Wrote two paragraphs, deleted one, rewrote it. What I have now is decent but there's a line in it that says "my work interrogates the boundary between observation and belonging" and I don't know if that's true or if it's just the kind of thing you're supposed to write. It sounds like everyone else's statement.

My father texted asking if I could come to Sunday lunch. I said yes immediately even though I was planning to photograph canvases this afternoon. Because you don't say no to Sunday lunch. You just don't. But now I've lost four hours I needed and I'm sitting here resenting something I actually want to do, which feels wrong.

Nudge: "What would happen if you said 'next week instead'?"

Sunday lunch was actually good. Papa made his dauphinois and we talked about the collective's funding application. He doesn't understand art but he understands budgets, so he went through the numbers with me and caught two errors in our projected costs. I felt useful sitting next to him with the laptop out, the two of us going line by line.

On the drive home I realised something. The reason I keep taking on the coordination work isn't just that nobody else will do it. It's that when I'm managing things, I'm in charge. People ask me. People defer to my schedule, my decisions about the shared space. If I step back and just become one of five artists in the collective, I lose that. I'm just another painter hoping for a gallery spot.

That's an ugly thought and I almost didn't write it. But it's true. When Sylvain says the collective would fall apart without me, part of me likes hearing it. Not because I want to do admin forever but because it means I matter in a way that doesn't depend on whether my paintings are good enough. Which is the one question I keep avoiding.

Nudge: "So what would 'good enough' actually look like?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe getting into something like the Prix Dauphine. Maybe selling a painting to someone who isn't a friend of my parents. Honestly I'm not sure that's the right question because the bar keeps moving.

Just got off the phone with Maman. She mentioned that my cousin Emilie got a promotion at her firm. Regional manager now, at thirty-one. Maman didn't compare us directly but she said "she's done so well" in that particular tone. Then she asked how the open studio planning was going, not how my painting was going. Because the planning is the part she can understand.

I'm tired of being the one in the family who chose the impractical path and then compensates by being responsible about everything else. I iron my clothes, I pay bills on time, I coordinate a collective of four adults who can't share a printing press without a mediator. And somehow it still doesn't add up to enough in certain conversations.

Finished the artist statement. Photographed all ten pieces with Sylvain's camera -- told him it was for the collective website, which is technically true since two of them are on there. Application is drafted. Just need to press send.

Submitted the Prix Dauphine application six days ago. Haven't told anyone except writing it here. Results aren't until December so there's a long stretch of not-knowing ahead. I've been filling it by throwing myself back into the collective stuff -- finalised the open studio weekend schedule, ordered new ink for the press, had a long call with the Mairie about our lease renewal.

Part of me knows I'm using the admin to avoid sitting with the fact that I put my work out there and it might not be enough. But another part thinks this is just what I do: I keep things running while I wait. It's not avoidance if the work genuinely needs doing. Or maybe I'm making excuses. Hard to tell from the inside.

Oriane asked at the studio yesterday if I'd ever considered applying for anything. I said "maybe at some point" and changed the subject to the November schedule. She nodded and moved on. I could have told her. I chose not to. It felt safer to be the person who organises things than the person who wants things. There's less to lose that way, or at least that's what I keep telling myself.

Helped Amira debug the intake form tonight at the nonprofit. She'd been stuck on a date-parsing issue for two days and we sorted it in twenty minutes. Small thing, but it felt like the right use of a Tuesday evening.

Nudge: "What made that feel like the right call?"

Response: Dunno, just better than watching Netflix again.

Team standup today. Felix made a comment about one of our logistics partners using subcontracted drivers with no fixed contracts, basically gig workers with zero protections. He said it casually, like it was just a business fact. I sat there and said nothing.

It's not like I didn't notice. I noticed immediately. But the meeting had twelve people in it and the product lead was driving the agenda and I just... let it slide. Afterwards I thought about saying something to Felix privately but then I got pulled into a deployment issue and the moment passed. I keep telling myself I'll bring it up tomorrow but I probably won't. It's easier to pretend I didn't hear it clearly.

Nudge: "What stopped you — the room, or something else?"

Response: Honestly the room. Twelve people, nobody else flinched.

Booked a flight to Lisbon for August. Budget airline, the one that keeps getting flagged for its emissions record. I know this. I looked at the train option and it was fourteen hours and three times the price and I just couldn't justify it.

I'm tired of every small decision feeling like a referendum. The flight was sixty euros. The train would've eaten half my weekend travel budget. I picked convenience and cost and I'm sitting here feeling vaguely bad about it, which is stupid because one flight isn't going to tip the scales. But it adds up and I know it adds up and that's the part that nags at me.

Lunch with Felix and Sandra. Sandra was talking about her new apartment in Schwabing and how she's glad the neighbourhood "hasn't changed too much," meaning it hasn't gotten more diverse. She didn't say it outright but the implication was clear. Felix nodded along.

I changed the subject to the upcoming sprint deadline. Could have said something. Should have, maybe. But Sandra is senior to me and she's leading the Q3 roadmap review. There's a calculation that happens in these moments and I went with the safe option. Finished my Kartoffelsalat and went back to my desk.

The carbon audit thing. I finally got a response from Stefan in compliance. He said the numbers I flagged were "within acceptable reporting tolerances" and that the methodology was approved by their external auditor. End of conversation, apparently.

So I looked into it more. The tolerance band they're using is laughably wide — basically you can underreport by fifteen percent and still be compliant. It's technically legal. But the whole reason I pushed for the audit was because I thought the company would want to know the real numbers. Turns out they already know and they're fine with it. The system is designed to allow this.

I could escalate. Take it to the sustainability board or even the Betriebsrat. But I've been here eighteen months and I'm not in a position to make waves. And honestly, part of me is relieved that Stefan shut it down because now I have an excuse to stop. That's the part I don't like admitting. I chose the easier path — I got my answer, I can file it away, and nobody has to be uncomfortable. Including me.

The thing is, when I left the adtech job I told myself I wouldn't do this. I wouldn't just accept things that feel wrong because it's inconvenient to push. And here I am doing exactly that.

Nudge: "You said you felt relieved — what does that tell you?"

Response: That I want someone else to fight this one. Which is exactly the problem.

New contractor started on the team. Guy named Tomasz, Polish, here on a project-based contract. During onboarding someone made a joke about Eastern European work ethic — meant as a compliment, apparently. Tomasz laughed it off. I didn't say anything.

It's such a small moment. Probably wouldn't even register for most people. But I noticed the way Tomasz's laugh didn't quite reach his eyes and I thought about saying something after the meeting and then I didn't. Went back to my Jira tickets instead.

Nudge: "What would you have said to Tomasz?"

Response: Maybe just that I noticed. That he doesn't have to laugh at stuff like that here. But saying that out loud feels presumptuous, like I'm deciding what should bother him.

Weird day in a good way. The nonprofit had their summer meetup at the Englischer Garten, just food and talking, nothing structured. I ended up sitting with this Syrian family I'd helped with their paperwork back in March, the Haddads. The dad, Marwan, was telling me about his daughter's school report — she got top marks in maths and he was so proud he was practically vibrating.

And there was this moment where he grabbed my arm and said something in Arabic to his wife and she smiled and he turned back to me and said "she says you are part of the story now." I didn't really know what to say to that. Just sat there eating my Falafel feeling like, okay, this is something real. Not a dashboard metric. Not a compliance tolerance. An actual family doing better because a few people showed up consistently.

I think about the work stuff — the carbon numbers, the things I don't say in meetings — and then I think about this afternoon and it's hard to hold both at the same time. But today was just good. Marwan's daughter got good grades and he wanted to tell me about it. That's enough for one Monday.

Had a one-on-one with my manager Katharina. She asked how I was finding the team culture and I actually said something. Told her the comment during Tomasz's onboarding bothered me and that I think we could be more careful about that kind of thing. She nodded and took a note and said she'd "keep an eye on it."

Not exactly a revolution. But I said it. Out loud. To someone with authority to do something. I've been replaying the conversation all evening trying to figure out if she took it seriously or just filed it under "Lukas is being sensitive." Probably overthinking it. The point is I didn't let it pass this time.

People at work probably think I'm easygoing because I don't argue in meetings. But easygoing and silent aren't the same thing. I've just been choosing silent for too long. Katharina's response wasn't dramatic — no promises, no action items — but she heard me. And I can live with that being the starting point.

Part of me wants to dismiss this as barely doing anything. Like, congratulations, you mentioned something mildly uncomfortable in a private meeting, what do you want, a medal? But no. I know what it cost me to say it and I know I wouldn't have done it two months ago.

Grocery run after work. Standing in Edeka staring at the coffee shelf. Fair trade option is almost double the price of the store brand. I know what the price difference means — it means someone down the supply chain is getting squeezed. I know this. I've read about it. I've shared articles about it.

Bought the store brand anyway. Forty euros left until payday and I'm not going to pretend that doesn't matter. But it's the same calculation I keep making. The flight to Lisbon. The compliance thing at work. There's always a reason to pick the easier option and the reason is always money or comfort or not wanting to be the difficult one.

I'm exhausted by the gap between what I think matters and what I actually do when it comes down to it. It's not even big moral failures. It's coffee. It's a budget airline. It's staying quiet at lunch. But it's all the same muscle and I keep not using it. And I'm too tired tonight to figure out what to do about that. Going to bed.

Nudge: "Is the gap getting bigger or smaller?"

Response: Honestly can't tell anymore. Some weeks smaller, some weeks it swallows everything.
Can't sleep. Keep thinking about how easily I let things go at work. Tomasz caught my eye in the hallway today and just nodded and I felt like a fraud.

Monday again, back on the bike by six. The app was slow this morning, kept waiting near Baner for orders that didn't come. Sat under a neem tree scrolling through other riders' complaints on the WhatsApp group. Same story everywhere — fewer orders, more riders, the algorithm deciding who eats. Literally. One guy said he made eighty rupees in three hours. That's not even petrol money.

Got a decent run after ten though, four back-to-back deliveries to those new tech park offices. The watchman at one of them made me use the service entrance and wait in a corridor that smelled like paint thinner. The guy who ordered — maybe twenty-three, twenty-four — took the bag without looking at me and closed the door. I don't know why that one stuck with me today. It's not new. Maybe because Ananya asked me last night what I do all day and I didn't know how to make it sound like something.

Meera had chai ready when I got back. She asked how much I made. I said decent. It wasn't, but she's already worried about the school fees due next month and I didn't want to add to that. Amma was watching her serial in the other room. Normal evening. Felt like holding my breath all day and only exhaling when I parked the bike.

Nudge: "What would you have told Ananya if you had the words?"

Response: She's eight, yaar. She just wanted to know if I drive fast. Kids don't need the whole picture.

Sunday distribution went well. We had enough packets for everyone at the metro site this time. One of the workers, a guy from Bihar, asked if we could bring blankets next week because the rains are starting. I said I'd ask the group. Walking back to the auto I kept thinking about how he said it — quietly, like he'd practiced asking so it wouldn't sound like too much.

School fees notice came. Fourteen thousand for the term plus two thousand for the activity kit. Meera put it on the fridge without saying anything. I did the math in my head at dinner and came up short by about five thousand. Could pick up extra night deliveries but the roads after ten are bad in monsoon. Amma asked why I wasn't eating. I said I wasn't hungry.

Nudge: "What happens if you tell Meera the real number?"

Response: She already knows. She always knows. She put the notice on the fridge instead of handing it to me — that's her way of saying we need to talk about it without actually saying it. I just haven't figured out what to say back yet.

Spent the morning fixing a leaking tap for Mrs. Kulkarni in B-wing. She always pays on time and gives me chai and biscuits, which honestly I appreciate more than she probably knows. Her grandson was there, studying for some entrance exam, books spread across the entire dining table. She told me he wants to get into IIT. She said it the way people say it — like it would save the whole family. I nodded. My father used to say the same things about my engineering entrance.

Didn't crack the entrance, obviously. Ended up in logistics. Then the warehouse. Then this. It's not a failure exactly, it's just — the life that happened instead of the one that was planned. I don't think about it much anymore but watching that kid hunched over his chemistry textbook brought it back. The pressure. The samosa breaks. The night before results when I couldn't sleep.

Afternoon was all deliveries. Nine orders, decent tips on two. The app sent me to NIBM twice which is far but the restaurant there packs well so nothing spills. Got home, helped Ananya with her Hindi homework. She writes her matras wrong still but she's getting there. Meera made bhindi. Amma complained it was too oily. Standard evening. Sometimes standard is fine.

Saw a news clip about the metro construction workers' camp being cleared for the next phase. The group chat blew up. Where do they go now? We've been bringing food there for months. Vinod-bhai said we should write to the municipal corporation. I said yes but I also know how that goes. Letters disappear. Meanwhile people get displaced and nobody even reports it.

Nudge: "What would actually make a difference there?"

Response: I don't know, honestly. Sometimes I think the food packets are just us making ourselves feel useful while the real machinery rolls over everyone. But then I think about that guy from Bihar who asked for blankets and how he looked at us like we were the only ones who noticed him. Maybe that's the difference. Not fixing the system. Just not pretending the people in it are invisible.

Meera's brother called today. Offered me a warehouse job at his friend's logistics company in Chakan. Steady salary, PF, the works. Thirty-minute commute if traffic cooperates, which it never does. The catch — it's a night shift, eight PM to four AM, six days a week. Meera wants me to take it. Her face lit up when she told me, like she'd been waiting for a rope to appear.

I should be grateful and part of me is. But I keep thinking about the Sunday distributions. Night shift means I'd sleep through Saturday mornings which is when we prep the packets. And I'd basically never see Ananya on school days. She'd be asleep when I leave and at school when I wake up. Meera says we adjust. Amma says a man provides. Everyone has an answer except the one I actually need — how do I hold everything without dropping something.

Nudge: "What are you most afraid of dropping?"

Response: The school fees aren't going to pay themselves, no? That's not a philosophical question, that's just math.

Meera and I talked properly after Ananya went to bed. She asked why I'm hesitating about the Chakan job. I told her I need to think. She said I've been thinking for two days and what's there to think about — it's a salary, it's fixed, it's what we need. She's not wrong on the facts. She's never wrong on the facts. That's the frustrating part.

I tried to explain about the Sunday group, about the metro workers being displaced, about how I can't just stop showing up when things are getting worse for them. She looked at me the way she does when she thinks I'm being impractical. She said "those people have problems we can't solve, Arjun, we have our own." And I know that. I do. But there's something in me that can't accept that the answer to our problems is to stop caring about theirs.

Went out to the balcony after. Sat on the plastic chair and looked at the street for a while. Auto-rickshaws, dogs, a man selling corn on the corner. Normal Pune night. I kept turning it over — steady paycheck versus everything else I'd lose. Meera isn't asking me to give up anything, she'd say. She's asking me to be practical. But practical has a cost she doesn't see because she's not the one standing at that metro site watching people get erased.

Told Meera I'd do a trial week at Chakan. Compromise. She seemed relieved. I also told Vinod-bhai I'd still come for distributions when I could and he said they'd manage either way, no pressure. That stung a little. Like the whole operation could run fine without me. But maybe that's a good thing. Maybe I needed to hear that it's not all on my shoulders.

Nudge: "What did you want Vinod-bhai to say instead?"

Response: Nothing different. He said the right thing. I'm just being sensitive about it. Moving on.

First week at Chakan done. The work is fine. Scanning parcels, loading trucks, shift manager seems decent. The money is real — I could feel it when I looked at the first week's calculation. Meera was right about that. The PF deduction alone felt like a small wall being built between us and the next emergency.

But I haven't seen Ananya properly in six days. She drew me a picture of a butterfly and left it on my pillow. Meera told me she asked why Baba isn't home for dinner anymore. I read her a story before leaving last night — she was half asleep, kept closing her eyes while I read, and I rushed through the last page because I had to catch the bus. That felt wrong. Not just inconvenient. Wrong. Like I was choosing a payslip over being present for the small things that make up a childhood.

Sunday distribution happened without me. Vinod-bhai sent photos to the group. They managed. New volunteers even showed up. And the metro workers' camp — it's already half gone. Fenced off. The remaining families are squatting near the nullah now. I keep looking at those photos thinking I should be there, but instead I'm forty kilometers away counting boxes for someone else's profit. Meera says it'll get easier. Amma says this is what men do. I don't know what I say yet. I'm too tired to figure it out tonight.

Nudge: "Is this the wall you wanted to build?"

Response: No. But it's the one I can afford to build right now. The butterfly drawing is on the fridge next to the school fees notice. That's my whole life on one fridge door. I don't know how to make both sides of it happy. I just keep showing up and hoping the math eventually works out to something that feels like enough.

Another sprint planning meeting where they want me to estimate the same maintenance tickets I have been doing for three years. I know exactly how long everything takes now. That is maybe the problem.

Nudge: "What's bothering you about knowing?"

The new architect they brought in, Jennifer, she wants to rewrite the payment gateway from scratch. Using Go instead of Java. Everyone in the room looked at me like I was supposed to object, since I built the original thing in 2019. But honestly? Part of me wanted to say yes, let us do it, let me learn something new instead of patching the same codebase forever.

I did not say that though. I said we should consider the timeline and the risk to the December release. Which is true. The business needs stability. But I walked out of that meeting feeling like I had swallowed something heavy.

Nudge: "You sided against yourself in there?"

Response: It is not that simple. Jennifer does not know what she does not know about the regulatory requirements. Someone had to be the adult.

Elena surprised me with tickets to a tech conference in San Francisco next month. Not the corporate kind with vendor booths and networking bingo, but one of those unconference events where people just propose sessions in the morning and see what happens. She said she noticed I have been looking at conference websites lately but never registering for anything.

I should be excited. Five years ago I would have booked the flight before she finished her sentence. Now I am already thinking about the sprint commitments, about whether Marcus can handle the on-call rotation alone, about what my manager will say when I request the days off. The conference is on a weekend. I would only need to take Friday off. Still, I hesitated.

What bothers me most is not the hesitation itself. It is that I used to make decisions faster. Move to Austin? Two weeks to decide. Take the startup job over the stable bank position? One evening with Elena and a bottle of wine. Now I research, I weigh, I consider all stakeholders. My mother would say I finally grew up. But it feels more like something got dimmer.

I will go to the conference. I already told Elena yes. I just wish the yes had come easier.

Nudge: "When did decisions start feeling heavy?"

Response: Maybe around the time my father got sick. 2018. Suddenly there were consequences everywhere I looked. Every choice felt connected to whether I would be there, whether I was reliable. He passed in 2019 and I thought it would lift, but it did not. The weight just found new things to attach to.

Long day. Production incident at 3 AM, the payment gateway again. Not the part Jennifer wanted to rewrite, ironically, but the legacy integration with the bank's SFTP server. I was on call so I got the page. Took four hours to trace it to a certificate expiration that nobody had on their calendar.

By 7 AM I had fixed it and documented everything. By 8 AM I was in the standup explaining what happened. By 9 AM I was getting messages from the VP asking how we let this happen. Not thank you for catching it. Not good job on the quick fix. Just how did we let this happen.

I did not let anything happen. I inherited this system. I have been asking for budget to modernize the certificate management for two years. But I did not say that either. I just said we would add better monitoring and put the renewal dates in a shared calendar. Safe answer. Team player answer.

Elena asked me at dinner why I looked so tired. I said work was busy. I did not tell her about the VP message because then she would get angry on my behalf and I would have to either agree with her anger or defend the company that made me feel small today. Both options felt exhausting.

I need to sleep but my mind keeps rehearsing the incident like I missed something. I did not miss anything. The certificate was not in any documentation I had access to. But I keep checking anyway.

Nudge: "You keep swallowing things down lately."

Response: Maybe I do. It is easier than the alternative. Every time I push back, I become the difficult one, the senior engineer who cannot adapt, the old guy who thinks he knows better. So I absorb it. I fix things quietly. I make myself small so the team can function. It is what my father did at his factory job for forty years. I used to think he was weak for that. Now I understand he was just tired.

The morning after a bad night is always strange. Everything looks normal. Coffee tastes the same. I just feel like I am watching myself go through the motions from somewhere slightly outside.

Nudge: "What motions are you watching yourself do?"

Response: The usual routine. Checking Slack before I get out of bed. Reviewing the overnight alerts. Logging into the standup. Answering questions I have answered a hundred times. It is not bad, exactly. It is just automatic. Like I am not really needed there, just my pattern.

Flew to San Francisco yesterday for the unconference. Staying at a hotel near the Embarcadero with a view of the bay. Weather is cold and gray but I brought my good jacket.

The conference format is interesting. You show up in the morning, people pitch sessions on index cards, everyone votes with stickers, and then the schedule emerges. No predetermined speakers, no approved slides. Someone proposed a session on rebuilding legacy systems with LLMs. Someone else wanted to discuss leaving tech after 50. I almost proposed something about maintaining software versus maintaining yourself, but I did not have the index card ready in time.

I attended four sessions. The legacy systems one was good, practical advice I could use. The leaving tech one was harder to sit through. A woman around my age talked about how she realized she had optimized her whole life for a company that laid her off in an afternoon. She cried a little. Everyone was respectful.

Tonight I walked along the waterfront instead of joining the dinner group. Watched the ferries come and go. Called my mother. She asked if I was eating well and I said yes, even though I had skipped lunch. Some lies are kindness.

Tomorrow is the last day. I feel different here. Not better or worse. Just different.

Nudge: "What stopped you with that index card?"

Response: I was still figuring out what I wanted to say. It felt too personal for a room of strangers. Maybe next time.

Back from the conference for a week now and people at work keep asking how it was. I say it was great, very inspiring, lots of good ideas. Which is true. But when they ask what I am going to do differently, I do not have a good answer. Learn Go? Propose a rewrite? Those feel like someone else's moves.

My manager suggested I do a lunch-and-learn to share what I learned. I said I would think about it. I probably will not do it. Public speaking was never my thing, and honestly, the sessions that meant most to me were the ones I cannot really present in a corporate setting. Nobody wants a slide deck about existential uncertainty in late-career engineers.

Nudge: "What did you actually bring back from there?"

Response: Some business card contacts. A reading list. The usual conference stuff.

December sprint started with three people out sick and a scope increase nobody agreed to. I have been working ten-hour days trying to keep us on track for the holiday release. Jennifer is frustrated because her Go rewrite keeps getting deprioritized. I am frustrated because I am the one who has to explain why to her without making leadership look bad.

Had dinner with Elena's cousin Miguel who is visiting from Monterrey. He runs a small construction company there. He asked me about my job and I tried to explain sprint planning and stakeholder management and he just laughed and said it sounds like I spend a lot of energy managing people's feelings about work instead of actually working. He did not mean it unkindly but it stuck with me. Maybe he is right. Maybe that is what corporate software engineering becomes after a while.

Nudge: "What would actually working look like for you?"

Response: Building something. Seeing it run. Not writing status reports about why it is not done yet. But that is not realistic at my level, I suppose.

I finally did it. I proposed to my manager that I move to Jennifer's team for the Go rewrite project. Not as a lead, not as an architect. Just as a developer. Someone who writes code and learns the new language and maybe remembers what it felt like to not know everything about a codebase.

He looked at me like I had asked for a demotion. Which, technically, I had. He said he would need to think about it, talk to HR, consider the implications. He asked if everything was okay at home. I said yes, everything is fine, I just want to learn something new before I am too old to learn.

I do not know why I said it like that. I am not too old. But something in me wanted to say it out loud, the fear underneath. Like naming it would make it less powerful.

Elena was proud of me when I told her. She said she has been waiting for me to do something like this for years. I did not know she was waiting. I thought I was hiding the restlessness well enough. Apparently I was only hiding it from myself.

Now I wait to see what they decide. Maybe they say no and I stay in my maintenance role forever. Maybe they say yes and I flounder in a new language at 57. Both possibilities feel more alive than certainty.
They said yes. Contingent on finishing the holiday release first, but yes. I start on Jennifer's team in January. I have been studying Go tutorials at night, feeling like a student again.

Amna presented the quarterly vendor review in our Monday meeting. She used my slides. Not the template I made for the department, the actual slides I prepared last week for the director call. She changed the title and rearranged two charts and acted like it was something she had been working on all weekend.

I sat there watching her walk through my analysis of the Faisalabad supplier delays. The phrasing was mine. The framing was mine. She even used the same footnote about container shortages that I pulled from the logistics brief. Nobody else in that room would have noticed because nobody else reads the logistics briefs. But I noticed.

I didn't interrupt. I didn't raise it. My boss nodded along and thanked her for the thorough breakdown. Amna didn't look at me once during the whole thing. That told me she knew exactly what she was doing.

Afterwards I went back to my desk and just sat there for ten minutes. I should have said something in the room. Something small, even. "I think I had similar findings in my director prep." But I froze. I keep replaying it and the thing that bothers me most is not that she did it. It's that I let her.

Nudge: "What stopped you in that moment?"

Response: It wasn't the right setting. You can't start calling someone out in front of the whole team, it would look petty. If I bring it up now it's old news. It's fine. I just need to make sure it doesn't happen again.

Submitted the new vendor onboarding proposal last Tuesday. It's been seven days and nothing from the director. No feedback, no questions, not even a "received, will review." I followed up once on Thursday with a polite message, kept it short, said I was available to discuss at his convenience. Read receipt came through within the hour. Still nothing.

The proposal took me three weeks. I mapped the entire onboarding cycle, identified two bottlenecks where we lose four to five days on compliance checks, built a revised timeline that would cut new vendor integration from six weeks to three and a half. This isn't a suggestion doc. This is a full operational redesign with cost projections.

Meanwhile he had time yesterday to sit with Bilal for forty minutes about the office renovation budget. Bilal is the facilities coordinator. The office renovation is new carpeting and a break room microwave. My proposal would save the company roughly twelve lakh per quarter in faster vendor activation. But sure, let's discuss the microwave first.

I can't push harder without looking desperate. I can't escalate because there's nobody to escalate to except him. So I sit and wait and pretend I have other priorities while the most important project on my desk collects dust in someone else's inbox.

Nudge: "What's harder — the waiting, or having no way to speed it up?"

Response: Having no way to speed it up. Waiting I can handle if there's an end date. This is just open-ended silence. I could walk into his office and ask directly, but then I look like I'm hovering. The proposal speaks for itself. He should have responded by now.

Farah pushed back on the dispatch scheduling changes in front of the whole floor today. She said the new system creates too much pressure on the packing team and that I hadn't consulted them before rolling it out. She wasn't wrong about the consultation part, but the way she said it — standing there with her arms crossed, loud enough for the warehouse guys to hear — it felt like she was making a point that had nothing to do with dispatch schedules.

I backed down. Told her we could revisit the timeline and get input from the packing leads. The words came out reasonable but inside I was furious. The old schedule had three missed shipments last month. Three. I fixed that. She's been here longer than me and I think she resents that I'm the one making these calls now. But I said fine, let's revisit. Because fighting in the middle of the floor would have made me look worse, not better.
Long day. Eid shopping with Ammi after work, then came home to finish the weekly reports. My eyes hurt and I still need to review tomorrow's vendor call brief before sleeping.

Bilal got the conference room booking system changed so department heads need to reserve through his assistant now. Used to be we could just block time directly on the calendar. He sent the email to all managers like it was a building-wide policy but I checked with procurement — it's just him adding a layer because someone double-booked the main boardroom last month.

It's a small thing. I know it's a small thing. But it means if I need the room for a client call at short notice, I have to go through his person first. I already sent a reply asking whether department heads with client-facing responsibilities could retain direct access. Kept it professional. He replied with a smiley face and "let's discuss offline." That means no.

I could go to the director about it but that feels like escalating a room booking dispute, which is exactly the kind of thing that makes a young manager look like she's overreacting. Ammi always says pick your fights carefully. She's right. But the small things add up and each one is a little bit of ground lost.

Nudge: "What ground are you worried about losing?"

Response: The ability to move fast without asking permission. That's the whole reason the dispatch overhaul worked — I didn't wait for committees and approvals, I just did it. Every new process that adds a gatekeeper between me and getting things done slows me down. It's not about a room. It's about whether I can operate at my own pace or everyone else's.

Had a client visit today. The buyer from the UK account came for the factory walkthrough and the director introduced me as "one of our operations team." Not operations manager. Not the person who restructured the entire dispatch flow that got them their last three shipments on time. Just "one of our operations team."

I smiled and shook hands and walked them through the floor like nothing happened. The buyer asked good questions about our quality checks and I answered every single one without hesitating. By the end she was directing her questions to me, not the director. That felt like something. But the introduction stung. I'm not "one of" anything. I run that floor.

Nudge: "She ended up directing questions to you — does that make the introduction matter less?"

Still thinking about yesterday. Mentioned it to Abba over dinner and he said I should be grateful to have the position at all at my age, that most girls from our family are still finishing their degrees or married by now. He didn't mean it badly, I know that. He's proud of me in his way. But "grateful" is not the word I want to hear when someone erased my title in front of a client.

I wanted him to be angry for me. Instead he told me the story about how his boss at the textile mill passed him over for promotion three times before finally giving it to him. Like patience is the lesson. Like waiting is the same thing as earning. I've already earned this. I just need the people around me to see it the way I do.
Vendor call went smoothly, secured better terms on the denim lot. Farah actually came to me afterwards and said the new scheduling format is working. Small acknowledgment but I'll take it.

Director finally responded to the onboarding proposal. Three weeks of silence and then a five-line email: "Good effort. Let's pilot with one vendor. Loop in procurement." No mention of the cost projections. No acknowledgment that I spent three weeks mapping the entire cycle. "Good effort" — like I'm an intern who turned in a decent assignment.

I should be happy. He approved it, technically. But "loop in procurement" means I lose sole ownership. Now it's a cross-functional initiative, which sounds collaborative on paper but really means I'll be coordinating with people who didn't build this and don't understand the logic behind the sequencing. I built something precise and now it gets diluted. I keep telling myself a pilot is better than no response. But I wanted him to see the full scope and say, "This is exactly what we needed."

Nudge: "What would 'enough' recognition look like for you?"

Cousin's engagement party last night. Every aunty asked what I do and when I said manager they looked confused, then asked about marriage plans. One of them said to Ammi, "At least she's keeping busy."

Nudge: "When they said that, who did you want to correct — them, or yourself?"

Response: Them. Obviously them. I don't need correcting. But you can't lecture your khala at a mehendi about what operations management means. So I smiled and ate my biryani and talked to my cousin about her lehenga instead. Some rooms aren't worth the fight.

Back from the Mekong research trip with my students. Three weeks of river sampling, village visits, and improvised lessons under thatched roofs. My body is wrecked—mosquito bites on my ankles, a persistent cough from the humidity, and I think I pulled something in my shoulder carrying equipment. But here's the thing: I feel more awake than I have in months.

The kids were incredible. There was this moment when we were testing water quality near a fishing village, and one student started asking the local fishermen about changes they'd noticed over the years. No prompting from me. She just did it. That kind of spontaneous curiosity—you can't manufacture that in a classroom with fluorescent lights and standardized tests.

Now I'm back at my desk, and already I can feel the walls closing in. Tomorrow's faculty meeting will be about exam preparation timelines and classroom decoration guidelines. I know I should be grateful for the stability of this position, but part of me is already googling "environmental education programs hiring abroad."

I need to sleep for about a week. But I also know that if I stay still too long, this feeling of being alive will fade, and I'll be left with the same restlessness I've been running from since I was thirty.

Nudge: "What happened at thirty?"

Faculty meeting was worse than expected. Three hours on compliance paperwork. Considered walking out twice.

Nudge: "What almost made you walk?"

Got coffee with an old colleague who left teaching to do documentary work—she's filming a piece on coral restoration in Indonesia, asked if I'd be interested in consulting. Said I'd think about it but we both knew that meant yes.

Nudge: "So why say you'd think about it?"

Response: Habit, I guess. I've learned not to show my cards too quickly. People get concerned when you seem too eager to drop everything. My mother used to call it "chasing the wind"—not a compliment. So I've gotten good at pretending to deliberate while my mind is already packing bags.

The coral project is moving forward. My colleague Mei-Hua sent over preliminary details: two months in Sulawesi, starting in March, helping design educational components for local schools. The pay is modest but includes accommodation and meals. My current contract here allows for unpaid leave if approved by the department head.

I spent most of Sunday creating a detailed proposal for why this should be considered professional development rather than abandonment. Listed educational outcomes, skill acquisition, potential for future curriculum integration. The proposal is twelve pages long. I know Principal Huang will see through it—he's commented before that I seem to have "one foot out the door"—but I need the formality to protect my position in case I want to come back.

The strangest part: I'm not even sure I do want to come back. But I keep building these safety nets anyway. Maybe it's the years of my father telling me that a respectable career means staying in one place, building a reputation, becoming someone people can count on. Every time I leave, I hear his voice asking when I'll finally settle down.

Submitted the proposal this afternoon. Now I wait.

Nudge: "What would it take to not build the net?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe if I had enough money that coming back wasn't a financial necessity. Or maybe if I stopped caring about what my family thinks. But I'm 48 years old and I still want my father's approval, even though he's been dead for six years. That's probably the most honest thing I've written in months.

Principal Huang approved the leave request—but with conditions. I have to document everything, submit weekly reports on "educational outcomes observed," and return with a "concrete implementation plan for classroom integration." Basically turning an expedition into a bureaucratic exercise.

And yet I said yes immediately because two months in Indonesia is two months not here, not in this building with its humming fluorescent lights and scheduled bathroom breaks for students and the same conversations about test scores over and over—

My colleague Li-Na said she envied me at lunch today. Said she wished she could just pick up and go like I do. I wanted to tell her it's not freedom, it's compulsion. I don't choose to leave; I have to leave, or something in me starts to suffocate. But that sounds dramatic and she has two kids in elementary school and a mother-in-law with dementia, so I just smiled and said "you should try it sometime" which felt like a lie and a truth at the same time.

The approval notice is pinned to my corkboard. March feels like forever away. I'm already looking at flights, making lists, thinking about what equipment I'll need. The anticipation is almost as good as the going. Almost.

Nudge: "You said compulsion, not choice. What's the difference for you?"

Response: Choice implies I could stay if I wanted to. That staying is a real option I'm evaluating. But when I'm honest with myself, staying was never on the table. The only question was whether I'd leave with permission or without it. Compulsion is when the leaving isn't optional—it's just a matter of how much I'll sacrifice to do it.

My sister called to say I'm being selfish again. That our mother is getting older and needs family nearby. That I "can't keep running away from real life."

Nudge: "What did you say back?"

Response: I hung up. But what I wanted to say was: this IS my real life. The running isn't away from something—it's toward something. The problem is I've never been able to explain what that something is, not even to myself. All I know is that when I'm moving, when I'm somewhere new, when I don't know what tomorrow looks like—that's when I feel like I exist. Staying in one place feels like slowly disappearing. But try explaining that to someone whose definition of a good life is stability and proximity to family.

Spent the morning at the travel clinic getting vaccinations for Indonesia. Typhoid, hepatitis boosters, antimalarials. The nurse asked if this was my first time traveling to the region and I laughed—it was rude but I couldn't help it. She looked at my vaccine record and said "oh, you're one of those."

One of those. I know what she meant. The perpetual travelers, the people who seem allergic to staying put. She probably sees us all the time in a travel clinic. I wanted to ask her what else she's noticed about "people like me" but that seemed like a conversation neither of us wanted to have.

Afterward I walked through the night market near my apartment, the one I've been to a hundred times. Normally it bores me—same vendors, same food, same crowds. But today I looked at it differently, knowing I'll be gone in six weeks. The elderly woman selling scallion pancakes. The teenagers playing games on their phones while manning their parents' stalls. The specific quality of the light through the plastic tarps.

I do this every time I'm about to leave: suddenly appreciate what I've been ignoring. Then I leave anyway.

Mei-Hua called with updates on the Indonesia project. The funding came through, the local partners are confirmed, and there's even been interest from a university in Singapore about documenting our work for a larger research initiative. This is everything I wanted.

So why do I feel this undertow of anxiety?

I've been sitting with this feeling all day, trying to name it. It's not fear of the unknown—the unknown is the part I crave. It's something else. Maybe it's that I've built my entire identity around chasing the next horizon, and I'm starting to wonder what happens when I stop. Not if, but when. Because at some point, the body slows down. The opportunities shrink. The world gets smaller whether you want it to or not.

My mother is 76 this year. My sister was right about one thing: she won't be here forever. And I've spent most of my adult life being somewhere else, chasing experiences I can't even fully remember now. I have photo albums from twenty countries, but I couldn't tell you the name of my neighbors.

I don't know if this is wisdom finally arriving or just fatigue dressed up as self-awareness. But for the first time in a long time, I'm not sure the leaving will fix what the staying breaks.

I'll go to Indonesia anyway. Of course I will. But I'm writing this down so I remember: there was a moment, in January 2025, when I wondered if there might be another way to feel alive.

Nudge: "What would that other way look like?"

Response: I genuinely don't know. That's the terrifying part. My whole life has been organized around movement—every decision, every relationship, every job has been filtered through "will this let me keep moving?" I've never seriously tried to find out what the alternative would feel like because it seemed like death. But maybe it's not death. Maybe it's a different kind of life that I've never given a fair chance. I just don't know how to start when standing still has always felt like drowning.

Stayed late at the lab again. Got home after the kids were asleep. Dina didn't say anything but I could tell from the way she left my dinner on the counter instead of the table. That's her signal.

The thing is I know I should've been home earlier. Layla had her first swimming lesson and I told her this morning I'd try to make it. "Try" is doing a lot of work in that sentence. I didn't try. I got pulled into running another simulation batch and the hours just disappeared. And now my kid learned to float without me there and I can't get that back. I keep telling myself the PhD is for them, for our future, but some of these moments don't wait for dissertations to be finished.

Nudge: "Which moments are you most afraid of missing?"

Response: Honestly all of them. But the ones where they stop asking me to come. That's what scares me.

Monday again. Advisor meeting at 10, went fine I think, he wants the literature review chapter revised by Friday which is tight but manageable. Spent the afternoon in the lab calibrating the shake table sensors. Boring but necessary work, nobody else was going to do it.

Dina took Omar to the pediatrician because he's had this cough for two weeks. She texted me the update: nothing serious, probably allergies, they gave him a prescription. I should've gone with her but Mondays are impossible with the advisor meeting and the TA prep for Tuesday's section. She handled it fine. She always handles it fine. That's partly the problem I guess, she's so capable that I lean on her for everything outside the lab and at some point that stops being partnership and starts being something else.

Called my mother in the evening. She asked when I'm defending. When I said probably next spring she went quiet then started talking about my cousin Faisal who just got promoted to department head at the ministry back home. He's two years younger than me. She didn't say it outright but the comparison was loud. Told her I had to go because Omar needed a bath, which was true but also convenient.

Nudge: "What does it start being?"

Response: Just imbalance I think. Not worth spiraling about right now.

Second time writing today which is unusual. Just got back from putting the kids to bed. Layla wanted me to read to her and I did three chapters of that Junie B. Jones book she's obsessed with. Omar fell asleep on my lap halfway through. Small thing but I needed it.

Dina and I actually talked after. Not about logistics for once. She told me about this cooking class she wants to take on Saturdays, Palestinian dishes from her grandmother's recipes. Her eyes lit up describing it. I told her she absolutely should and I'd handle the kids Saturday mornings. She looked surprised, like she wasn't expecting me to just say yes. That bothered me a little because it means she's gotten used to me being unavailable.

But I said yes and I meant it. Saturday mornings are mine with the kids and hers for the class. This is something I can actually hold onto. The dissertation won't collapse because I take three hours on a Saturday. And Dina deserves to have something that's just for her, same way the PhD is my thing. Though calling the PhD "my thing" feels strange when it's supposed to be for all of us. Whatever, the point is tonight felt right. I was present and it didn't cost me anything I'll regret.

Pulled two all-nighters this week to get the chapter revision done. Submitted it at 4am. Body is wrecked. Dina asked if I was okay and I snapped at her, said I was fine when I obviously wasn't. Feel awful about that. She doesn't deserve my stress dumped on her.

Nudge: "What were you actually feeling when you snapped?"

Response: Guilty. Because she was being caring and I resented it somehow. Like her concern reminded me I'd been neglecting everything that wasn't the dissertation.

Apologized to Dina this morning properly. Not just a mumbled sorry but sat down with her during the kids' cartoons and told her I knew I was being unfair. She said she knows the pressure I'm under but she needs me to not take it out on her. Fair. Completely fair.

Made pancakes for everyone after. Layla put chocolate chips in hers in the shape of a smiley face. Omar ate his with his hands and got syrup everywhere. Normal family morning and I was actually there for it instead of hunched over my laptop. Advisor can wait until tomorrow.

New month. Advisor approved the revised chapter with minor edits. Felt good to get that confirmation after the crunch. Started outlining the methodology chapter, should be more straightforward since it's describing work I've already done.

Dina started her Saturday cooking class last week. She came home with maftoul and it was better than my mother's, which I will never say to my mother. The kids and I went to the park while she was gone. Taught Layla to pump on the swing by herself. Omar mostly ate sand. Standard Saturday. This arrangement is working and I want to protect it.

Lab mate Chen got accepted to present at the ASCE conference in November. Good for him. His advisor fast-tracked the submission. Mine hasn't even looked at my abstract draft from three weeks ago. I know I shouldn't compare timelines. Chen doesn't have kids, doesn't have a family depending on him, works weekends without guilt. Different situation entirely. But sitting in the lab watching him celebrate while I'm still grinding through Chapter 3 revisions, I couldn't help doing the math on how far behind I am.

Dina asked how my day was and I said fine. Didn't mention Chen. Didn't want to sound petty about it.

Nudge: "What does 'behind' mean to you here?"

Response: Just timeline stuff. Conference papers, publications. The CV metrics that actually matter for the job market.

Layla asked me tonight if I'm going to be a student forever. She wasn't being mean, just genuinely confused. Told her almost done. But the question rattled around in my head after. Am I taking too long? Am I dragging this out because finishing means facing what comes next?

Nudge: "What comes next that worries you?"

My mother called and dropped the news that my father's been having chest pains. He went to the doctor last week apparently and nobody told me until now. When I asked why, she said they didn't want to "worry me during my studies." I'm 38 years old and they're still treating me like I'm a kid who can't handle bad news.

I'm angry but I also know exactly why they did it. Because every time I talk to them I'm stressed about the PhD, about money, about the kids. So they stopped telling me things. They shielded me and now I feel like I failed them by being so consumed with my own problems that they couldn't even lean on me. Dina found me sitting in the kitchen at midnight staring at my phone. She just sat with me and didn't say anything for a while. Then she said we could look into flights for winter break if I wanted to visit. She's already thinking about logistics while I'm stuck in my own head.

I told my advisor I might need to go to Amman for a few weeks. He said it's fine as long as I keep writing. Of course that's what he said. Everyone needs something from me and I can't figure out how to be enough for all of them at once.

The Hamburg hub finally hit ninety-six percent on-time and I didn't even get a mention in the quarterly call. Kramer presented the numbers like they were his. Sat there chewing my pen while the board nodded along.

Nudge: "What stopped you from speaking up during the call?"

Response: Politics. Kramer reports to the CEO directly. Picking a fight on camera would only hurt me.

Still stewing over the call. Lotte opened a bottle of that 2019 Brunello we'd been saving for something and I couldn't even enjoy it properly. Kept replaying the moment where Kramer said "the regional team" instead of my name. The regional team. Like the numbers came out of nowhere.

It's not just vanity, though people would say it is. When you run three sites and you restructure the nightshift rotation yourself and you push back on corporate for months to get the new sorting line funded, having your name attached to the outcome matters. It's how you move up. It's how people know who to call when the next VP seat opens.

Nudge: "What happens if the VP seat opens and they don't think of you?"

Response: Then six years of building this region into the best-performing in the company would have been a waste. And I'd have to seriously think about whether I stay or find somewhere that notices.

Two of my warehouse leads called in sick on the same day and I spent six hours on the floor sorting parcels like it's 2003. My back is wrecked. Cancelled dinner reservations at that new place in Katendrecht because I couldn't face sitting upright for two hours.

Had the one-on-one with Kramer today. Brought up the quarterly call, kept it measured. He said he appreciated my "team-first attitude" and that everyone knows who does the real work. Classic deflection. He even patted me on the shoulder on the way out, which I found condescending but smiled through anyway.

Lotte says I should document everything from now on. She's probably right. She also said I need to stop letting work ruin my evenings, because last weekend I spent the entire Saturday sailing trip checking emails on my phone instead of actually being out there. She's definitely right about that one. I barely noticed the water.

Nudge: "You said you barely noticed the water. How often does that happen — being somewhere you chose to be but not really being there?"

Response: More than I'd like. But that's the deal when you run things, isn't it.

Got the green light to hire two senior coordinators for the Antwerp hub. My budget, my picks, no committee. Finally something that feels like actual authority instead of managing upwards all day. Already have a shortlist.

Hosted twelve people for dinner last night. Lotte paired every course — she did the lamb with a Châteauneuf-du-Pape that made van der Berg's wife close her eyes when she tasted it. That's the kind of evening I live for. Good food, the right people, candles on the canal, Lotte in her element. Van der Berg even asked if we'd consider doing a supper club. I said maybe, but honestly I don't want to turn it into an obligation.

The thing is, half the guests were people from the industry. Not a coincidence. You build your network over a cheese course, not in a meeting room. Van der Berg sits on the advisory board at Port of Rotterdam. His wife runs procurement at Maersk Benelux. I like these people genuinely — I do — but I also know exactly what those relationships are worth. Lotte says I can never just have fun without an angle. She's not wrong, but I don't see why it can't be both.

Nudge: "When Lotte says you always have an angle — does that bother you or does it feel like a compliment?"

Response: Depends on the day. Sometimes I think she sees through me in a way that's useful. Other times it makes me feel like I can't just enjoy something without her questioning my motives.

Long week. The Antwerp expansion hit a snag — permits delayed, which means the two coordinators I hired start in three weeks with nothing structured for them to do. I've been on calls with the municipal office, with legal, with the construction firm, trying to unstick things. None of it is technically my job. The site manager should be handling permits. But if I leave it to him we'll lose another month, and I can't afford that with the VP shortlist conversation supposedly happening in Q3.

Lotte and I were supposed to go to Bruges for the weekend. She booked a hotel with a rooftop terrace and a Michelin-star restaurant for Saturday night. I told her I'd need to work Saturday morning and she went quiet in that way she does. Not angry, just — recalibrating. She went anyway, took her friend Anneke. I stayed home and spent the day on a laptop in my bathrobe eating cheese toast. By four o'clock I realised the permit issue was probably going to take another two weeks regardless of whether I spent Saturday on it or not.

I could have been in Bruges. I could have been sitting on a rooftop with a glass of something excellent, watching the sun go down with the person I like most in the world. Instead I ate cheese toast. I don't know what to do with that information.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to have gone to Bruges instead?"

Lotte showed me photos from Bruges. The terrace, the food, the canal at sunset. She wasn't rubbing it in — she just wanted to share. I looked at the photos and felt something I can only describe as stupid. Not sad. Stupid.

Permits came through. Both coordinators start Monday and the fit-out begins next week. I walked the Antwerp floor this morning and rearranged the staging layout myself because the site manager's plan was going to create a bottleneck at dock three. He didn't love me overriding him but he'll see the throughput numbers in two weeks and come around.

Took Friday off. Actually took it off — phone in a drawer, sailed out to the Grevelingenmeer with nobody but the cats in my head for company. Grilled fish on the dock afterwards, drank a cold Riesling, watched the light change on the water for an hour. Didn't think about Kramer or permits or throughput once. Well, maybe once. Lotte would be proud. Didn't tell her about the once.

Nudge: "You mentioned the phone in the drawer like it was a big deal. Was it?"

Response: Yes. I check it probably forty times a day on weekends. Old habit from when I was on-call as a shift lead. Now it's just control.

VP shortlist leaked. Three names. I'm one of them. Kramer is not, which should make me feel vindicated but instead I just feel the weight of it. If I get this, I'm responsible for all five regions across the Benelux. Sixty direct reports through my managers. A seat at the executive table. Everything I've been manoeuvring towards for years.

But there's a clause in the offer framework that the role is based in Amsterdam and requires eighty percent in-office. Lotte's business is in Rotterdam. The canal house is in Rotterdam. The boat is twenty minutes from Rotterdam. I'd be commuting two hours a day or we'd need a second flat in Amsterdam, which means I'd be living half my life in a box near Zuidas eating takeaway alone while Lotte hosts wine tastings without me.

I keep doing the maths in my head. More status, more scope, more say in how the company runs. But also more time in transit, fewer evenings on the terrace, fewer dinners where Lotte pairs a wine so perfect it changes the conversation. I don't know how you weigh those things against each other. Every time I think I've decided, I flip. Lotte hasn't said what she thinks yet. I think she's waiting to see what I actually want before she tells me what she wants, and honestly that scares me more than the interview.

Nudge: "If Lotte told you she wanted you to take it, would that make it easier or harder?"

Response: Harder. Because then I couldn't blame the decision on protecting what we have. I'd have to admit I want it and deal with what that costs.

Had a call with Sami from the Aqaba branch about the delayed container shipments. Third time this month. He kept circling around the issue without giving me a straight answer about what went wrong on their end. I told him I need the incident report by Thursday and he agreed, but I could tell he was just saying what I wanted to hear.

Spent most of the afternoon reviewing Q1 numbers with Dina. She's been solid this quarter — her team's turnaround times are the best in the division. Told her I'd put her forward for the efficiency award at the next leadership meeting. She seemed surprised, which is odd. She should know she's earned it.

Picked up the girls from piano. Layla has her recital next week and she's been anxious about it — keeps saying she'll mess up the second movement. Sat with her after dinner and ran through the piece twice. She nailed it both times. She's better than she thinks she is but she won't believe me when I tell her.

Nudge: "What's actually going on in Aqaba?"

Response: Honestly I think Sami's lost control of his warehouse team. Two of his senior guys left in January and he promoted people who weren't ready. But if I say that out loud it becomes my problem to fix, and I've got enough on my plate.

Second time opening this today. Just got off the phone with my mother. She wanted to know if Fadi is "settling in okay" which is her way of asking whether I'm watching out for him. He's fine. More than fine — he closed the Zarqa supplier deal on his own last week and didn't even tell anyone until the contract was signed. I called him into my office this morning to tell him I noticed. The look on his face. Like he didn't expect anyone in management to actually see what he's doing day to day.

That's the thing that gets me. I remember being twenty-seven at my first real job and my manager barely knew my name. I told myself if I ever ran a team I'd make sure people felt seen. Not in some corporate poster way but actually noticed. Fadi's doing good work and he should hear it from someone who matters in the building.

Noor texted me a photo of Rania's math test — ninety-eight percent. Already thinking about how to celebrate at dinner. Maybe we do kunafa from Abu Salem's.

Nudge: "Why does that matter so much to you?"

Response: Because nobody did it for me. I had to figure everything out alone when I started. My first manager couldn't even remember which project I was on. I just don't want that for people under me.
Got an email from HR about the VP role. They want me to "onboard" the new guy next week. Show him around, introduce him to the team leads. My team.

People keep asking me how I feel about the VP situation like they expect me to be bitter about it. I'm not bitter. The man hasn't started yet and I'm already being asked to hold his hand through orientation. Fine. I'll do it. I'll do it well because that's what professionals do and because everyone will be watching how I handle it.

Had the onboarding meeting today. His name is Khalil. MBA from London, five years at Maersk. He's polished. Asked good questions. I answered them thoroughly and introduced him to every team lead personally. Dina told me afterward she was impressed by how generous I was with my time. That word — generous. I don't know if that's what it was. I know how things work. The person who trains the new boss controls the narrative.

Fadi called me tonight to ask about a contract clause he wasn't sure about. Spent twenty minutes walking him through it. He thanked me three times. I told him to stop thanking me and just get it right.

Nudge: "Was it generous or strategic?"

Response: Both. I genuinely want the transition to go well for the company. But I'm not naive — if I'm the one who made Khalil's first week smooth, that positions me a certain way. I don't see anything wrong with that.
Friday at home. Noor's at the clinic for a half day so I took the girls to Teta's house. My mother made mansaf and spent the whole lunch telling Rania she needs to eat more.

Khalil scheduled a "division alignment" meeting for Monday without telling me first. Found out from Dina who got the calendar invite. She assumed I already knew. I didn't. It's a small thing but it's the kind of small thing that tells you how someone sees the org chart.

Had lunch with Amjad from finance. He mentioned casually that there are rumors about restructuring — possibly merging operations and logistics under Khalil. I kept my face neutral but inside something tightened. I've built this team from twelve people to forty-two. The processes, the KPI framework, the vendor relationships — those are mine.

Came home and Noor could tell something was off. She asked and I said work stuff, the usual. She didn't push. She knows when to give me room. After the girls went to bed I sat on the balcony for a while thinking about what I'd actually do if they folded my division. Started going through my LinkedIn for the first time in months. Not seriously. Just looking.

Nudge: "How serious is the LinkedIn thing really?"

Response: It's not serious yet. I'm not updating my profile or messaging anyone. But I want to know what's out there in case I need to move fast. I refuse to be caught off guard.

Fadi came to me today looking shaken. Khalil pulled him into a meeting and started questioning the Zarqa deal — asking why procurement didn't go through the new approval workflow that apparently Khalil set up without consulting me or my team. Fadi didn't know what to say. He did everything by the book, by the process that existed when he closed the deal.

I went to Khalil's office and told him directly that if he's changing procurement workflows he needs to loop in operations first because my people are the ones executing these contracts. He smiled and said he appreciated the feedback. Appreciated the feedback. Like I'm filing a suggestion at a hotel.

I'm angry but what's worse is I feel like I let Fadi down. He came to me because he trusts me and I brought him into this company. I should have anticipated that Khalil would start making changes without understanding the existing structure. I should have shielded him from this. Now Fadi's second-guessing himself over a deal he should be proud of.

Nudge: "Could you have actually prevented that?"

Response: Probably not. Khalil was going to assert himself no matter what. But I could have warned Fadi, prepared him for the possibility that new leadership means new rules. I just didn't think it would happen this fast.
Layla asked me at breakfast why I've been "grumpy." I told her I wasn't grumpy, just busy. But she's right. I've been bringing it home and that's not fair to them.

The restructuring rumor turned out to be exactly that — a rumor. CEO called me in on Thursday and told me operations stays independent, reporting to him, same as before. He also said he relies on my institutional knowledge and wants me on the quarterly strategy committee starting next month. I nodded, said thank you, kept it professional. Walked out of his office and called Noor immediately.

Took the whole family to Abu Salem's on Friday night to celebrate — didn't tell the girls why, just said we deserved it. Fadi texted me that evening asking for advice on a lease negotiation for his apartment. Nothing to do with work. I spent thirty minutes on the phone with him going through the clauses. Noor rolled her eyes but she gets it. Family is family, even at nine p.m. on a Friday.

Supervisor meeting went fine. She said the lit review draft is solid but needs tighter framing around the methodology gap. Called Mama after, she asked when I'm coming home. Same conversation every Tuesday.

Nudge: "What does 'same conversation' actually sound like?"

Response: She asks when I'll visit, I say after the semester, she sighs, I change the subject to Noor's day at the shop. It's a script at this point. Anyway, the lit review feedback was useful — I should focus on that.

Three nights of maybe four hours' sleep each and I still didn't finish the conference paper draft on time. I emailed my supervisor asking for two more days and she replied with a single line: "Let's discuss priorities in our next meeting." I've read that sentence fifteen times. It could mean nothing. It could mean she thinks I'm falling behind. Both feel equally possible and I can't stop cycling through it.

Noor called tonight about the shop — the main printer broke down again and the repair estimate is more than what I usually send in a month. I told her I'd figure it out. Didn't tell her I've been eating rice and lentils for two weeks straight to keep my own rent covered. She doesn't need that. She's handling the shop and Papa's physiotherapy appointments and I'm over here unable to finish a twenty-page paper.

The worst part is I know people in my cohort half my age are publishing faster. Ahmed from the lab already has two journal submissions this year. I'm not jealous of him specifically, it's more that I keep measuring myself against a timeline that was never mine to begin with. I chose to come here late. I keep forgetting that.

Nudge: "You said you'd figure it out for Noor. Have you actually figured it out?"

Supervisor meeting was actually good. She wasn't upset — she wanted to talk about narrowing my scope so the paper is submittable by July. She drew this diagram on the whiteboard, crossing off two whole sections I'd been agonizing over. Felt like someone opened a window.

Walked home through the park after and sat on the bench near the pond for a while. Called Mama from there. Told her about the meeting and she said "habibi, you always worry too much." She's right, probably. Noor texted later that she found a cheaper repair guy through a friend at the souk. Small thing but I felt my shoulders drop reading it.

End-of-term presentations today. I went third. My slides were clean, my data was organized, I rehearsed four times this morning. And then during Q&A, Professor Chen asked a question about my sampling methodology that I couldn't answer clearly. I fumbled through something about stratified approaches and he just nodded politely. That polite nod — I know what it means. It means the answer wasn't good enough.

The frustrating thing is I knew the answer. I'd written about it in section four of my draft. But standing there with twelve faces staring at me, half of them twenty-six years old and already sharper on their feet, my mind just locked. I sat down after and my hands were shaking slightly. Nobody noticed, I think.

Came home and couldn't eat dinner. Opened the spreadsheet where I track my targets — publication by August, comprehensive exam in October, teaching assistantship application due next week. Every single line has a yellow or red flag next to it. I used to run project teams of forty people. I built a bridge overpass in Zarqa. And now I can't get through a fifteen-minute Q&A without my voice going thin. I keep telling myself this is worth it, that the degree changes everything, but tonight I'm not sure what "everything" even refers to anymore.

Nudge: "When you first decided to come here, what was 'everything' supposed to look like?"

Response: I think I pictured myself walking across a stage and my mother watching from the front row. Papa too, before the stroke. The degree was supposed to prove that leaving the career and the salary wasn't reckless — that I could build something bigger than a bridge. But now most days it just feels like I traded one kind of pressure for another, except this one pays nothing and nobody at home understands what a comprehensive exam is.

Quiet week for once. Submitted the revised paper draft, supervisor said it reads much better with the narrower scope. No major feedback, just a few citation formatting things. I spent Saturday catching up on the teaching assistantship application — recommendation letters are in, statement is drafted, just need to polish the research summary section.

Called home and for once it was a normal conversation. Mama talked about the neighbors' daughter's engagement party. Noor complained about a supplier who keeps shorting her on paper stock. Nobody asked me when I'm coming back. It felt different. Lighter.

I realized I've been here almost three semesters and I still eat dinner alone most nights. There's a departmental social on Thursday that I keep meaning to attend but I always find a reason not to. Last time it was the paper deadline. Before that it was needing to call home during the time slot. I'm not lonely exactly — I talk to my family every day and I see my labmates — but there's a gap between being around people and actually having someone here who knows me beyond my research topic. It's not urgent. It's just something I notice when the work quiets down enough to notice anything.

Ahmed asked me at lunch if I'd thought about switching to a more "applied" research track since I have industry experience. He meant it helpfully, I'm sure. But the way he said it — like the theoretical work is too much for someone who came in mid-career — sat with me all afternoon. I told him I didn't leave a perfectly good job to do a watered-down version of what I already know. He looked surprised. Maybe I was too sharp.

But I'm tired of the assumption that because I'm older and came from practice, I should be doing something "practical." I'm here because I wanted the harder version. I wanted to understand the theory underneath the things I built for twenty years. That's not a consolation prize, it's a deliberate choice. People keep framing it like I'm catching up when I'm actually trying to go deeper.

The thing I didn't say to Ahmed is that his question hit exactly where I'm already uncertain. The comprehensive exam reading list is dense and some of the mathematical modeling chapters make me read the same page four times. I am keeping pace, but barely. And when someone who's been in academia since undergrad casually suggests I take the easier route, it confirms the thing I'm afraid of — that maybe the gap between where I started and where I need to be is wider than effort alone can close. I don't actually believe that. But I didn't need him planting the seed.

Nudge: "You said you don't believe the gap is too wide. But you also didn't push back on the thought."

Good day. Actually good. Spent the morning working through chapter nine of the modeling textbook — the section on finite element analysis that's been haunting me for weeks. Something clicked today, like the pieces rearranged themselves while I was sleeping. I worked through three practice problems without checking the solutions until the end. Got two out of three right. The third was a sign error, not a conceptual one. That matters to me even if it sounds small.

Went to the departmental social after all. Stood in the corner with a cup of tea for the first twenty minutes but then Priya from the structures lab came over and we ended up talking for an hour about her fieldwork in Gujarat. She studies soil liquefaction. I told her about the overpass project in Zarqa and she asked genuinely interested questions — not the polite academic kind, the kind where someone leans forward. It felt good to talk about the work I did before as something that adds to what I'm doing now rather than something I left behind.

Walked home and the air smelled like rain. Called Mama and told her about the social. She said "finally, you're making friends" in that tone she uses when she's been worried but doesn't want to say so. I laughed. She's not wrong. Noor sent a photo of the shop with a new sign she designed herself — clean lettering, the family name in green. I saved it to my phone's home screen.

Teaching assistantship results came in. I got it. Starting September, I'll be a TA for the second-year structures course. The extra stipend means I can send Noor more without skipping meals myself, which honestly matters more to me than the line on my CV.

Sat with that for a while this morning. Three semesters of tracking every deadline on that spreadsheet, color-coding my progress, comparing myself to people who started this path twenty years before me. And here's a concrete thing that says: you belong here, you can do this, someone looked at your file and said yes. It doesn't fix the comprehensive exam anxiety or make the reading list shorter. But it's something solid to stand on.
