Called Mama tonight to tell her about the TA position. She cried. Then she told me Papa smiled when she repeated it to him. I don't know if that's true or if she said it for me, but I'll take it either way.

Nudge: "What would it change if it were true?"

Response: Probably nothing practical. He can't travel here, he can't come to a graduation. But it would mean he knows I'm not wasting this. That the years away and the money spent turned into something he can recognize. I think that's what I've been working toward underneath all the spreadsheets and deadlines — just him knowing.

Closed the Morrison deal today and the whole thing went exactly how I wanted it to. No compromises on timeline, no scope creep clauses they tried to sneak in, kept the IP ownership clean. Their legal team pushed back twice and I just waited. Didn't budge. They came around because they knew I'd walk and they needed me more than I needed them.

It's weird though, sitting here with the signed contract and a good whiskey, and I'm already thinking about what's next. Not in a restless way—more like I'm finally at cruising altitude and can look around. Eight years of building this thing on my own terms. Nobody telling me which clients to take or how many hours to bill. Worth every sleepless night in the early years.

Had dinner with my sister and her husband. Derek spent half the meal explaining how I should restructure the business, hire a COO, "scale properly." Like he's some genius because he climbed the ladder at a healthcare company and has a pension waiting for him. He doesn't get it. He'll never get it.

What really got under my skin was when Rachel nodded along with everything he said. She used to understand why I left corporate. Now she just sees the gray in my hair and the fact that I'm still single and makes assumptions. I'm not broken. I'm not running from anything. I just don't want someone else's version of success stapled to my forehead.

Nudge: "What would your version of success actually look like right now?"

Response: I mean, I already have it mostly. The Morrison deal, the freedom. Just gets old having to defend it every family dinner. Whatever.

Finally told Gary I won't be renewing the Nextera contract. Three years of dealing with their approval committees and change request bureaucracy—I'm done. The money was steady, but every time I had to sit through one of their "alignment meetings" I could feel my brain cells dying.

He didn't take it well. Called it impulsive. Said I was "burning bridges" and "thinking with my ego." Maybe. But I walked out of that call and felt like I could breathe for the first time in months. That has to mean something. I started this company so I'd never have to sit in another meeting where ten people discuss whether we need a meeting. Not going back to that. Not for any client.

Met with two potential clients this week. Turned down the first one—fintech startup with a founder who kept interrupting me to explain my own expertise back to me. Not worth the aggravation.

Second meeting went well. Small biotech company, three founders who actually listen. They want custom lab management software and gave me full creative control over the architecture. Already sketching out the data models in my head. This is the kind of work I got into business for. Clean problem, smart people, no corporate layers. Said yes on the spot.

Nudge: "What made saying yes on the spot feel right?"

Response: They treated me like an expert, not a vendor. Asked my opinion and actually wrote it down. No committee approvals, no "we'll circle back." Just three people who knew what they needed and trusted me to build it. That's rare.

Spent most of the day reading about database architecture I could've just asked someone about. Old habit—figuring things out myself even when it's slower. Not sure if that's stubbornness or something else.

Nudge: "What's the "something else" you're hinting at?"

Response: Pride, probably. The satisfaction of solving it on my own. When I ask someone else, even if it's faster, it feels borrowed. Like I didn't really earn the solution.

Made solid progress on the biotech project. Finished the database schema and got their sign-off on the first prototype wireframes. They're easy to work with—send questions when they have them, otherwise leave me alone to build.

This is exactly the rhythm I've been craving. Wake up, coffee, work on something that actually matters, nobody pinging me about status updates or asking for a "quick sync." I control the timeline. I decide when to push hard and when to step back.

Took a long walk this afternoon just to clear my head and found myself actually smiling. Not at anything specific—just at the fact that this is my life, that I built it, that nobody handed it to me or approved it for me. I know that sounds simple but sometimes you need to step back and notice it. Eight years in and I'd still rather do this with less money than go back to a cubicle with more.

Ordered Thai food and worked until midnight because I wanted to, not because anyone was expecting it. That distinction matters.

Haven't slept more than four hours in three days. Biotech project hit a wall—their legacy system is a mess and integrating with it is turning into a nightmare. I said I'd have a working prototype by January 5th and now I'm not sure that's realistic.

Part of me wants to call them and reset expectations. That would be the smart thing. But another part of me—the part that's been driving this whole operation—hates the idea of admitting I underestimated the scope. I quoted the timeline. I own it. Even if it means working through the holidays.

Caught myself snapping at the barista this morning over nothing. That's not who I want to be. But I also can't shake this feeling that if I ask for more time, or ask for help, or admit I can't do it alone... I don't know. Something shifts. The whole story I tell myself about being the guy who figures things out—maybe that story has a cost I don't usually see.

Rachel called to ask if I'm coming for Christmas dinner. Told her I'd try. We both knew I was lying. I could hear the disappointment but also something else. Resignation, maybe. Like she's stopped expecting anything different.

Nudge: "What would it cost you to ask for help right now?"

Back from the chaos. Prototype works—not perfect, but works. Sent it over this morning and they loved it. Called it "exactly what they envisioned" which, honestly, made the last two weeks worth it.

But I've been thinking a lot about what happened in the middle there. The exhaustion, the snapping at people, missing Christmas. I pulled it off, sure, but I pulled it off in a way that felt familiar in a bad way. Like I was back in my twenties, proving something to nobody in particular.

I called Rachel yesterday. Apologized for Christmas. She said she gets it, which is worse than if she'd yelled at me. Told her I'm going to try to make January different. Not sure what that means yet. Maybe it means being okay with asking for help sometimes. Maybe it means picking projects where the timeline has some breathing room. Maybe it just means sleeping before I become someone I don't recognize.

The thing is, I know why I do it this way. When I solve the hard problem myself, when I stay up three nights to hit an impossible deadline, there's this feeling—like I'm proving I can still do it. That nobody else is required. That the kid who walked out of that corporate meeting made the right call. But maybe proving it over and over is its own kind of trap. Maybe at 41 I could occasionally let myself be helped without it meaning I failed.

Anyway. Project's done. Taking the rest of the week off. Might actually answer the phone when Rachel calls.

Nudge: "What would "being helped" look like if it didn't mean failure?"

Response: I think it would look like having someone else in the room. Not an employee, not a contractor—someone who actually shares the weight. A partner, maybe. I've always said I don't want a co-founder because I don't want to compromise, but maybe what I'm really afraid of is depending on someone. Trusting someone enough to let them in. The business is the easy part. The loneliness is harder to admit.

Had my quarterly review today and Fatima said I exceeded expectations on every metric. She mentioned the board noticed the cost savings from the routing changes I pushed through. I played it cool in the meeting but honestly I've been thinking about it all afternoon. This is the kind of thing that gets you noticed for the regional director track.

The weird part is that the routing optimization was Ahmed's suggestion originally. I refined it and made it actually work, but still. I didn't bring that up in the meeting. He's been here eight years and reports to me — I'm not about to split credit and make myself look like I need hand-holding from my own team.

Nudge: "You refined it, but you know whose idea it was."

Response: Ahmed's got job security either way. I don't.
Spent the weekend at Atlantis with Youssef and Khalid. Pool day Saturday, Nobu Saturday night, recovery brunch Sunday. Needed that reset after the push to close Q1 numbers.

I'm so tired of Rami second-guessing everything I decide. Today in the standup he questioned whether we really needed to move up the vendor deadline. In front of everyone. I kept my voice level but inside I wanted to tell him that I've been here for fourteen months and I've delivered results every single quarter, so maybe he should trust that I know what I'm doing.

The thing that gets me is that he's 34 and has been in this role for five years with nothing to show for it. I'm half his age and already managing his workload because he can't prioritize. But I'm the one who has to justify my decisions? It's exhausting. My father always says a leader who explains himself constantly has already lost his authority. I'm starting to understand what he meant.

After the meeting I pulled Rami aside and told him if he has concerns, he can bring them to me privately. He apologized but I could tell he didn't mean it. Whatever. I'm not here to be liked. I'm here to deliver.

Nudge: "That's your father's rule. Is it yours?"

Response: I don't know. I've never really separated the two. My whole life, the men I respected ran their households and their businesses the same way — you make decisions, you don't ask permission, people follow or they don't. That's what being in charge looks like. But sometimes I wonder if they were actually happy, or if they were just... in control. Those aren't the same thing. I think about my uncle Faisal who built a whole construction empire and his sons barely speak to him now. He's still the boss though. So did he win?

Team dinner tonight, my pick, chose that new Lebanese place on JBR because I wanted good food and I wanted people to see us there. Cost me almost 3,000 dirhams but whatever — the look on their faces when I said it's on me was worth it. Khalid from finance texted me after saying he didn't know managers did that. I told him real leaders invest in their people.

Except now I'm home and my account is going to be tight until the 25th. The car payment comes out Thursday. I'll figure it out. Always do. Maybe I'll skip the boat party this weekend. Or maybe I'll just put it on the card and deal with it later. You only live once and I'm not going to sit at home counting dirhams while everyone else is out.

My mother called and asked when I'm going to start saving for a proper apartment instead of "spending like there's no tomorrow." I told her I'm building relationships that will pay off later. She doesn't understand how things work here. You can't network from a studio in Sharjah.

Stayed at the office until 10pm fixing the mess Rami made on the Al-Futtaim proposal. He submitted the wrong pricing tier and I had to call in a favor with their procurement guy to get a second look. It worked — we're still in the running — but I'm running on fumes.

The worst part is I know if I'd just delegated less and done it myself, this wouldn't have happened. But then everyone says I micromanage. So I step back and this is what I get. It feels like I can't win. Either I control everything and get called difficult, or I trust people and they drop the ball.

Ordered sushi to the office and ate alone at my desk. Not exactly the Thursday night I planned. Youssef sent photos from the rooftop at Five — I should've been there.

Nudge: "What would your Thursday have looked like if nothing went wrong?"

Response: Gym after work, quick shower at home, meet the guys atCia by 9, maybe end up at White or somewhere on the marina. Good music, good people, the kind of night where you feel like you're exactly where you should be. Instead I'm eating salmon rolls under fluorescent lights. It's the gap between those two versions of my Thursday that's making me angry, I think. Not Rami specifically.

Took myself to brunch alone today. First time in months I didn't organize it for a group or invite anyone. Just me, a table by the pool at Sofitel, unlimited sparkling and eggs royale. It felt strange at first — I kept checking my phone like I was waiting for someone. But by the third glass I was actually relaxed in a way I haven't been in weeks.

I've been thinking about why I always need an audience. Even good things feel less real if no one sees them. Like, if I have a nice meal alone, did it even happen? That's probably not healthy. My grandfather used to take long walks by himself every evening and he seemed at peace. I can't imagine doing that. The thought of unwitnessed time makes me uncomfortable.

But today was good. The food was good. The sun was good. Maybe I don't need everyone to know I was there. Maybe the experience is enough. I'm not convinced yet but I'm thinking about it.

Nudge: "What would you lose if no one saw?"

Response: Proof, I guess. That I'm living the life I'm supposed to be living. If no one sees it, how do I know it's real? How do I know I'm not just some guy eating alone because he has no one? The photos, the posts, the reservations at places with waitlists — that's evidence. Without it I'm just... trusting my own experience to be enough. And I don't know if I trust that yet.

Got the regional recognition for Q2 results. Fatima presented me with a plaque at the all-hands. Took a photo with the CEO. Already thinking about what's next.

Nudge: "You got what you worked for. How does it feel?"

Cancelled on Sara for the third time this month. She said she understands but her voice was flat. I told her work is crazy right now and she just said "it's always crazy." She's right. I don't know what to tell her.

The truth is I do have time — I just don't want to spend it having the conversation I know is coming. She wants to know where this is going. I want to keep things light. Those are incompatible. But I also don't want to lose her. She makes me look good. We look good together. That matters in this city.

I know that sounds terrible. She's also smart and funny and I like being around her. It's not just about appearances. But I can't say appearances aren't part of it either.

Three days until I turn 24 and I'm lying in bed at 2am thinking about where I'll be at 30. Regional director? Easy, if I keep this pace. But then what? I've been chasing this ladder since I was 19 and I've never actually pictured what's at the top. A corner office? More money? More people asking for my approval?

Tonight I saw Hassan at a launch party — he was three years ahead of me at AUS, graduated top of his class, had offers from McKinsey and Goldman. Now he runs a small dive shop in Fujairah. He looked... relaxed. Happy maybe. We talked for ten minutes and he didn't mention his job once except to laugh about how his commute is now a five-minute walk to the beach.

I thought I'd feel sorry for him. Instead I felt something closer to envy and I don't know what to do with that. His life is small by every metric I care about. No status, no visibility, probably makes less than my car payment. But he's not performing anything. He's just there, doing what he wants, and it seems like enough for him.

I can't imagine that being enough for me. And yet the fact that it bothers me is bothering me more.

Got a notification from the app that they're changing the pay structure again starting next month. Third time in a year. The base rate drops and they say the bonuses will make up for it, but last time they said that, my weekly take dropped by almost eighty mil. I wrote it down, I have the numbers.

Lina said don't worry about it yet, wait and see. But I can't just wait and see. I ran the numbers in my notebook during a break between deliveries and if it's like last time I'd need four extra hours a day to match what I'm making now. Four hours I don't have.

Nudge: "What's the backup if those numbers hold?"

Stupid decision. Signed up for a third delivery platform because somebody at the parqueadero told me it pays well on weekends. Had to put down a deposit for their branded thermal bag — ninety mil — and now I'm juggling three apps on my phone at the same time and I can't keep track of which order belongs to which one.

Picked up a Rappi order thinking it was the new app and drove twenty minutes in the wrong direction before I realized. Lost the time and the gas. Lina asked how my day was and I just said fine because I didn't want to explain why I'm now paying for a third bag I might not even use past this week.

The thing is, I already have a routine that works. Two apps, I know the zones, I know which restaurants are slow, I know where to park. I broke my own system trying to grab a little more and now I'm scattered. Should have just stayed with what I had. The ninety mil bothers me more than it should — it's not huge, but it's money out the door for something I didn't need.

Nudge: "What made this week different?"

Response: Wasn't different really. Somebody just talked it up and I moved too fast. It happens.

Dropped the third app. Deleted it this morning, not worth the mental load. Back to the two I know. Lost the deposit but whatever, lesson paid for.

Regular Thursday route. Picked up from the arepas place on Carrera 7, three runs to Chapinero, one long one out to Usaquén that paid decent. Stopped at the tienda on the way home and Lina had saved me a plate of arroz con pollo from lunch. She'd had a slow afternoon — said the distributor raised prices on cooking oil again, which means she either eats the cost or marks up and risks losing her regulars. We talked about it for a bit. Not much to do about it.

Logged my earnings in the notebook. Slightly above average for a Thursday. Bike needs new brake pads soon, I can hear them starting to grind. Priced them out at the shop near the house — forty-five mil, which is fine. I'll do it Saturday when I'm off.

Week's been rough on the bike. The brake pads I was going to replace Saturday — didn't do it. Saturday got busy, Sunday Lina needed help at the tienda, Monday I forgot, and now it's Thursday and they're grinding metal on metal. I can feel the vibration through the handlebars when I stop.

I know it's not smart. Every day I ride with bad brakes is a day something could go wrong, and fixing a bent rotor costs ten times what pads cost. I've been through this before, years ago, ended up paying three hundred mil because I waited too long. You'd think I'd learn.

But here's the thing — stopping for the repair means losing half a day of work, and this week the app has a streak bonus if I hit thirty deliveries by Friday. I'm at twenty-two. If I stop tomorrow to fix the bike I lose the streak and probably two hundred mil in bonus. So I keep telling myself one more day, one more day. Lina doesn't know the brakes are this bad. She'd tell me to fix them immediately, and she'd be right.

Nudge: "When does one more day stop making sense?"

Fixed the brakes last Saturday, finally. Fifty-two mil with labor because I waited so long the rotor needed resurfacing. Could have been forty-five if I'd gone when I planned, but it's done and the bike stops clean now. Feel better on the road.

My cousin Andrés called, wants me to invest in a food truck he's putting together with some guys from his old job. Says I could put in two million and be a silent partner and make it back in three months. I told him I'd think about it but honestly I already know the answer.

People act like I'm afraid of opportunity. I'm not afraid. I just know what happens. Andrés has had three business ideas in two years and none of them lasted past the first quarter. Two million is two months of savings. It took me a year and a half to build what we have in that account and I'm not going to watch it disappear into a food truck that parks in a bad location and folds by August. Lina agrees with me but says I should at least hear him out so he doesn't feel dismissed. Fine, I'll have cafecito with him. But the money stays where it is.

Nudge: "What's the hardest part of saying no to him?"

Response: Honestly it's that he'll think I don't believe in him. Which — I mean, I don't, not with money. But he's family. I don't want him going around telling everyone I'm tacaño. I just can't be the one who funds his experiments.

App glitched during peak hours and didn't register three of my deliveries. That's maybe sixty mil gone. I submitted a support ticket but those take days and half the time they just say the system shows it correctly on their end. Hate depending on something I can't control.

Took a shortcut through Suba today because Waze said it would save twelve minutes and I had back-to-back orders. Road was torn up, construction, no signs, just orange cones everywhere. Had to loop back and ended up twenty minutes late on the delivery. Customer gave me one star. One star because the city can't put up a proper detour sign.

Normally I stick to my routes. I have them memorized, I know which streets flood when it rains, I know where the potholes are. I don't need Waze. But I was behind and I got greedy with the time and now I have a one-star rating dragging down my average. If it drops below 4.7 they reduce your priority in the queue which means fewer orders which means less money.

That's what gets me — one bad rating from one shortcut I didn't need to take and now I'm spending the next two weeks being extra careful and extra polite trying to claw back points. All because I didn't just take the route I know. Lina says I obsess over the rating but she doesn't understand how the algorithm works. It's not just a number, it's whether I eat next month.

Nudge: "Has a bad rating ever actually cost you work?"

Response: Not yet. But I've seen it happen to guys at the parqueadero. One of them dropped to 4.5 and his orders went to nothing for a month. I'm not going to wait to find out firsthand.

Wednesday. Steady day, nothing unusual. Eleven deliveries, which is about average for a midweek. The app paid out the streak bonus from last week, finally — showed up in my account this morning, a hundred and thirty mil. Transferred it to savings immediately before it sits in the checking account and turns into groceries.

Lina's tienda had a decent day too. She sold a bulk order of panela to a neighbor who's making natilla for a birthday party this weekend. She was happy about it, said it was the biggest single sale this month. We had dinner together for once — usually I'm still on the bike at eight but today I finished early.

Checked the notebook. Month is tracking slightly above April, which is good. If I keep this pace through the rest of May I can cover the bike insurance renewal in June without dipping into savings. That's the goal. One month at a time.

Long day but the kind that feels right at the end. Fourteen deliveries, almost all in Chapinero which is my best zone — short distances, good tips, restaurants that have the food ready when I arrive. My rating came back up to 4.82. Took two weeks of clean runs but it's back where it should be.

Paid the bike insurance early. Lina looked at me funny because it's not due until June 15 but I told her I'd rather have it done. One less thing on the list. She gets it, she does the same thing with the tienda's distributor invoices — pays them the day they arrive even though she has thirty days. We're the same that way, both of us want the ledger clean.

Andrés never brought up the food truck again. I think he found someone else or maybe the whole thing fell apart. Either way I'm relieved. The savings account is at the highest it's been since December, and Lina and I are talking about maybe finally fixing the bathroom plumbing that's been leaking slow for months. Real things, things that last.

Nudge: "How long has the plumbing been going?"

Response: Since February more or less. It's a slow drip under the sink, not urgent, but the water bill crept up a little. I've been pricing plumbers — around four hundred mil for the job. We'll probably do it in June once the insurance is squared away. One thing at a time.

Ran the departmental meeting today and everyone stayed on time, on topic. New teacher Camila tried to bring up a textbook she liked and I redirected before it derailed things. Small thing, but it matters — I set the rhythm of these meetings and when they run well, the whole week runs well.

Nudge: "What was Camila's suggestion about?"

Response: Not the point. We had an agenda and I kept us on it.

Still thinking about that meeting. The truth is Camila looked a little deflated when I moved past her. But if I let every junior teacher pitch their favorite material mid-agenda we would never get through anything. The department works because someone holds the line. That someone is me and it has been me for fifteen years.

Nudge: "She got to you a little, didn't she?"

Response: She is new. She will learn how things work here.

Faculty meeting this morning, the big monthly one with the coordinadora and all department heads. I had prepared a ten-minute summary of our curriculum adjustments for next semester. Detailed slides, enrollment data by section, everything organized the way I like — no fluff, just facts and a clear recommendation.

I got three minutes in before the coordinadora cut me off. Said she wanted to "open the floor" for a more collaborative discussion. Then Profesor Ríos from the science department started talking about interdisciplinary projects and somehow my curriculum adjustments became a footnote to his pitch. He used my enrollment data — the numbers I had just shown — to support his own proposal. Nobody seemed to notice. Or maybe they noticed and did not care.

I sat there for the rest of the meeting contributing when asked but mostly just watching my work get absorbed into someone else's narrative. I wanted to say something. That those were my numbers, my analysis, my recommendation. But the coordinadora had already set the tone and pushing back would have looked petty. So I smiled. Nodded. Made a note in my planner to address it privately later.

Walking back to my classroom I could feel it in my jaw, the clenching. Fifteen years I have been building this department and Ríos, who has been here four years, gets to repurpose my work in real time while I sit there like I am attending someone else's meeting.

Spent the morning reorganizing the exam schedule for finals. Nobody asked me to — I just saw that the current draft from admin had conflicts that would affect my students. Fixed it, sent it to the vice-rector with my corrections marked in red, cc'd the relevant people. That is how you get things done around here. You do not wait for permission, you present the solution.

Nudge: "Why the red marks and the cc's?"

Regular Wednesday. Classes went fine. The eleventh graders are reviewing for their exam on Independence movements and most of them actually did the reading, which is a first this semester. Ate lunch at my desk grading quizzes while the other teachers chatted in the sala de profesores.

Nudge: "Why at your desk instead of the sala?"

Response: I get more done alone. The sala is just gossip and complaints about students.

Coming back to this because something happened after classes. The vice-rector stopped me in the hallway and thanked me for the exam schedule corrections I sent last week. Said it saved them a headache and that she was going to mention it at the next admin meeting. Small recognition but I will take it. That is how it should work — you do good work, someone notices.

It reminded me of something my father used to say. He managed a textile warehouse in Medellín for twenty-something years and he always told me the man who controls the calendar controls the operation. I did not understand it when I was young. Now I live it. Every week I review the departmental calendar, the exam schedule, the committee meeting dates. I know what is happening before most people know it is happening. That is not obsession, that is just how you keep things from falling apart.

My wife Patricia says I should relax more at home, that I bring the school energy into the house. Maybe she has a point. But when I relax, things slip. I have seen it happen to other department heads. They let go of the reins and within a semester the curriculum is a mess, the exam dates are wrong, and everyone is scrambling. I will not be that person.

Nudge: "What would actually slip if you let go?"

Third time writing today but Patricia's comment is still rattling around in my head. She is not wrong that I bring work home. But she married a man who takes charge of things. That is who I am at school and at the junta and at home. I do not know how to be half of that.

Sent the coordinadora an email Monday asking for approval to add a new elective on Colombian political history for next semester. It is Thursday and nothing. No reply, no acknowledgment. I know she read it because she responded to a facilities request I cc'd her on the same day. My proposal just sits there while I wait for someone who probably has not taught a class in a decade to decide whether my idea has merit.

Nudge: "The silence is saying something to you?"

Response: It is saying she does not respect the department heads enough to respond promptly. That is an organizational problem, not a personal one.

The coordinadora finally replied. She likes the elective idea but wants to "co-develop" it with the social sciences department, meaning Profesor Ríos gets to put his fingerprints on my proposal. I wanted to push back but she framed it as collaboration and you cannot argue against that without looking difficult. So I said sounds good. It does not sound good. My idea is about to become a committee project where fifteen years of expertise get diluted into consensus.

Priya arranged Arjun's birthday party venue without asking me. She just booked it and told me afterward, like I'm someone you inform rather than someone you consult. I didn't say anything. Too tired to start that conversation today.

Nudge: "What stopped you from saying something?"

Response: I didn't want to be the difficult one again. But it sits in my chest.

The plumber was supposed to come at ten and didn't show until two. I called the building secretary three times and he kept saying he'd "check and get back." Meanwhile the kitchen tap is dripping and I'm just standing there waiting for this man to decide when my house gets fixed.

Rajan said to be patient, these things take time. Easy for him to say — he went to his friend's place to play cards. I'm the one stuck here rearranging my whole afternoon around someone else's schedule. By the time the plumber actually arrived I was so frustrated I couldn't even explain the problem properly. Just pointed at the tap and walked away.

Normal Saturday. Made dosa for everyone in the morning, dropped the grandchildren at their Tamil tuition class, picked up vegetables from Ranganathan Street on the way back. The fish vendor kept the seer fish aside for me like he always does — he knows I come every week and he knows I won't accept anything less than the morning catch.

Spent the afternoon sorting through the steel cupboard. Found old report cards from when Vikram and Suresh were in school. Vikram always had better marks but Suresh was the one the teachers wrote nice things about. Funny how you remember these details decades later. Put everything back neatly and felt satisfied that at least one part of this house stays exactly as I organize it.

Sat with Arjun this evening and helped him with his geography project. He kept asking me questions about rivers and I actually remembered everything from when I used to quiz his father on the same chapter. He looked up at me and said "Paati knows everything" and I laughed but honestly it felt like the truest thing anyone has said to me in weeks.

Nudge: "Why did that hit so differently?"

Response: Because a seven-year-old doesn't say things to manage you. He just meant it. Everyone else in this house measures their words around me.

Writing again because something happened after dinner that I can't stop thinking about. Vikram and Priya want to enroll Arjun in a different school next year — some international curriculum place in Adyar. I said the school he's in now is perfectly fine, it's the same one Vikram attended, the teachers know our family. Priya started listing reasons — better labs, sports facilities, "global exposure," whatever that means.

I pushed back. I said I've seen these flashy schools come and go, and the fundamentals matter more than a swimming pool. Vikram just sat there quietly and then said "Amma, we've already applied." Already applied. The conversation wasn't a discussion. It was a notification dressed up as one. I looked at Rajan and he shrugged like this was none of his business.

I said fine. What else could I say? They've decided. I went to the kitchen and started washing vessels that were already clean because I needed to do something with my hands. The school is probably fine. That's not the point. The point is nobody thought my opinion was worth getting before they moved forward.

Suresh's wife Kavitha came over today and started rearranging my pooja room shelf. She said she'd seen a "better way" to organize it on some YouTube video. Didn't ask me, just walked in and started moving things. The brass Ganesha that my mother gave me, the kumkum box I've had for forty years — she was shifting them around like they were decorative items in a showroom.

I told her to stop. She looked surprised, like I was overreacting. Maybe to her it's just a shelf. But I've maintained that space for thirty-five years and every item is where it is for a reason. She doesn't get to walk in and rearrange my house because she watched a ten-minute video. I put everything back afterward and my hands were shaking but I'm not going to apologize for that.

Nudge: "What was the worst part of that moment?"

Response: It's done now. I put everything back. No point dwelling on it.

Family WhatsApp group. Vikram posted photos of houses he and Priya are looking at in OMR — those new gated community flats near the IT corridor. Everyone started commenting, congratulating, sending emojis. I found out my son is planning to move his family to the other side of the city through a group chat. Not a phone call, not sitting across from me at the dining table. A WhatsApp message.

I typed something and deleted it. Typed again and deleted it. Called Vikram directly and he said "Amma, it's just an option, we haven't decided." But the photos were of a specific flat, second floor, three bedrooms. That's not browsing. That's deciding. I told him if he's going to move he should at least tell me face to face and he said I was making it a bigger deal than it is. Maybe I am. But this is my son telling me through a phone screen that my grandson might not be ten minutes away anymore.

Rajan told me to calm down and that made it worse. I don't need to calm down. I need someone in this family to understand that decisions about where my grandchild lives are not minor updates you drop in a chat.

Nudge: "What did the deleted messages say?"

Response: The first one asked why I'm always the last to know. The second one said something about respect. I deleted both because I knew Priya would read them too and it would become a whole thing.

The new maid started today. The agency sent her without confirming the time with me first — they called Priya because she'd given her number when she initially contacted them months ago. So Priya told the maid to come at eight, which is right when I'm doing my morning prayers. I had to rush through everything because a stranger was standing in my hallway waiting for instructions.

I told Priya afterward that household staff coordination goes through me. She said she was just trying to help since I'd been complaining about not having help. That word — complaining. I wasn't complaining. I was stating that this house needs support and I expected to be the one organizing it. There's a difference between asking for help and handing over the reins.

Spent the rest of the morning showing the maid how things are done here. Where each vessel goes, how the kolam powder is stored, which rooms get mopped first. She was listening carefully, I'll give her that. But the whole morning felt off. Like I was being eased out of my own role without anyone saying it out loud. Rajan asked if I liked her and I said she's fine. He didn't ask anything else.

Quiet Tuesday. Made sambar, took Arjun to his Bharatanatyam class, argued with the auto driver about the fare — he tried to charge extra because of the rain and I told him I've been taking this route for ten years, I know the rate. He grumbled but drove.

Had a meeting this morning with the developer team in Bangkok about the app. We went over the feature backlog and prioritized the location-based discovery algorithm for the next sprint. The flight over was uneventful—grabbed the red-eye from Taipei so I could hit the ground running.

Afterward, I walked around Thonglor for a few hours. Found this basement bar that only opens after 11pm and doesn't show up on any maps. The owner is a former DJ from Berlin who moved here three years ago. We talked about how Bangkok's nightlife scene is evolving faster than Tokyo's right now. He introduced me to his network of venue owners. Could be useful for partnerships.

Spent the evening updating the pitch deck for next week's investor meeting in Singapore. The current traction numbers look decent but not explosive. Need to figure out how to make the growth story more compelling. Booked a hostel near Clarke Quay for the trip—the one with the rooftop bar where you can network with other founders.

Nudge: "That basement bar sounds wild. How'd you even find it?"

Response: Honestly, I just kept walking. I'd already been to the main spots everyone knows, and I was bored. Turned down some alley because I saw a guy carrying vinyl records disappear into a doorway. Followed him. Turns out the entrance is behind a laundromat. You have to know to knock twice. The owner said he doesn't want it on Google—says the moment a place gets discovered, it dies.

Got another call from my mom today. She forwarded me a LinkedIn post about my high school classmate Kenji who just made senior associate at McKinsey Tokyo. She didn't say it directly, but I could hear it in her voice—"See? He has stability. What do you have?"

I have three half-built companies and a carry-on bag. I have a network across four cities. I have ideas that keep me up at night. But sure, I don't have a business card with a respectable title or a steady paycheck.

The thing is, I'm not failing. The app has 2,000 beta users already. We're getting organic signups from word of mouth. But to her, none of that counts until there's a salary deposited every month. She doesn't understand that the salary is the trap. That's how they get you—comfortable enough that you stop looking for what else is out there.

I know she means well. I know she just wants me to be okay. But her version of "okay" feels like suffocation. Every time we talk, I have to defend choices that feel obvious to me. It's exhausting.

The investor meeting is in three days. If this round comes through, maybe that'll finally be proof enough. Or maybe it won't matter and she'll just find something else to worry about.

Nudge: "You said she means well, then called it suffocation. Which one sits heavier?"

Response: Both, that's the problem. I know she loves me and that's exactly why it hurts. If she didn't care, I could just ignore her. But she does, and her caring looks like wanting me to be someone I'm not. The suffocation is heavier, if I'm being honest. Because at least if she were indifferent, I'd be free.

Still thinking about that call. Went out tonight to clear my head—hit up three different rooftop bars with some founders I met last week. One of them is building a fintech play in Vietnam, another is doing sustainable fashion in Seoul. Neither of them has stable jobs either. Nobody questioned each other's choices.

Maybe that's why I surround myself with people like this. Not because I need validation, but because I need to be around people who get that the risk is the point. You don't build something new by playing it safe. And yeah, maybe some of us will fail—statistically, most of us will. But at least we'll know we tried something different.

Nudge: "Do those rooftop conversations ever make you feel like you're building your own family out here?"

Response: Ha, family is a strong word. We're more like... fellow travelers, I guess. People passing through the same checkpoint at the same time. Some of them I'll probably never see again after this month. That's fine though. I'm not looking for permanence.

Investor meeting didn't go the way I hoped. They liked the concept but wanted to see "more traction" before committing. Classic. They want to fund things that are already working, not things that could work. Where's the vision in that?

I tried explaining that nightlife discovery is a category that doesn't exist yet—you can't show hockey stick growth for something people don't know they need until they have it. But they kept asking about retention metrics and monthly active users. Numbers I don't have because we're still in beta. It felt like being graded on a test I wasn't given.

My co-founder suggested we pivot to something safer. Maybe restaurant recommendations or travel itineraries—stuff with proven demand. I told him if I wanted to build something boring, I would've just stayed in university. That conversation didn't end well.

Nudge: "How'd it end with your co-founder? That sounds tense."

Response: He said I care more about the idea being interesting than the idea being successful. Like those are mutually exclusive. We're still talking, but there's this distance now. I think he wants out but doesn't want to say it directly. Maybe I should just ask. Or maybe I don't want to hear the answer.
Co-founder officially left. Can't say I'm surprised. Starting over again with a smaller team and lower runway, but weirdly I feel lighter. At least now every decision is mine.

Why does everyone act like pivoting is failure? I've done it three times now and people look at me like I'm lost. I'm not lost—I'm searching. There's a difference. Lost means you don't know where you're going. Searching means you know the destination exists, you just haven't found the right path yet.

My aunt asked when I'm going to "settle down" with one business. Settle down. Like I'm supposed to marry a startup. Maybe the right one hasn't appeared yet. Maybe the point is to keep exploring until something feels like it can hold my attention for more than a year. Is that so wrong?

Nudge: "What would need to happen for something to finally hold your attention?"

Response: That's the million-dollar question, isn't it? I'll let you know when I figure it out. For now, I'm just trusting the process.

Couldn't sleep last night. Kept thinking about the runway—four months left, maybe five if I cut costs. The app is growing but not fast enough. Wondering if I should take on contract work to extend the timeline or if that would just distract me from the main thing.

Nudge: "Is it the money keeping you up, or something else?"

Response: The money is the easy answer. But honestly, it's more like... what if I'm running out of runway on me? Like, what if I've been chasing this feeling of building something new for so long that I've forgotten how to actually finish anything? That thought hit me around 3am and I haven't been able to shake it.

Spent the day wandering Shibuya without any plan. Haven't done that in months—usually I'm running between meetings or on a call with someone in a different timezone. But today I just walked. Watched people. Sat in a coffee shop for three hours. Read a book I've been meaning to finish since January.

There's this weird guilt that comes with unproductive time. Like I should be building or networking or optimizing something. But also... I felt more present than I have in weeks. Noticed things I usually miss. The way the light hits the buildings around 4pm. The sound of trains overhead mixed with street musicians.

I used to do this all the time in university—before I decided that was boring and left. Maybe the problem isn't that I need more novelty. Maybe I've just been looking for it in the wrong places. Or maybe I'm just tired and romanticizing a slow day because it's different from my usual pace.

Either way, it was good. I think I needed to remember that standing still isn't the same as being stuck.

Nudge: "What made today feel different from being stuck?"

Response: I chose it. That's the difference. When I'm stuck, it feels like the walls are closing in and I can't move. Today I could've moved—I just decided not to. There's freedom in that. The stillness was on my terms.

Had a call with a potential angel investor today. She's based in Hong Kong, built and exited two companies before she was 35, now advises early-stage founders in the region. She listened to my pitch, asked sharp questions, and then said something that's been echoing in my head since.

She said: "You clearly have energy. But energy without focus is just noise. What's the one thing you're willing to be boring about for the next five years?"

Five years. That's longer than any relationship I've had—with a person or a project. The question isn't whether I can handle it. It's whether I even want to. Part of me thinks that's the whole point: find the thing worth being boring for. Another part of me thinks that's just conformity dressed up in startup language.

I don't know. I told her I'd think about it. She said that's fine, but the market won't wait forever for me to figure myself out. She's probably right. She also said she'd check in next month to see where my head's at.

The runway isn't just money. It's time. And I'm spending both whether I have a plan or not.

Called my mom today—not because she called me, but because I wanted to. Told her about the investor meeting, the co-founder leaving, the uncertainty. She listened more than she lectured, which was surprising. At the end, she just said: "You'll figure it out. You always do. Just don't forget to eat."

It wasn't the conversation I expected. Maybe I've been so busy defending myself that I forgot she's also capable of just being my mom. Not the version of her I argue against in my head, but the real one who's worried and proud in equal measure.

Still don't know what I'm going to tell the angel investor next month. But I'm less afraid of the question now. Maybe the answer doesn't have to be forever. Maybe it just has to be honest.

First full week done at Birch Lane. The kids are sweet. One boy, Mateo, already told me he wants to read "chapter books like his sister." That kind of thing is why I'm here. The building is old and the supply closet is basically empty, but I spent the weekend printing worksheets at the library and laminating them myself. Cost me about forty dollars out of pocket, which I noticed when I checked my account this morning.

I set up a reading corner with pillows I found at a thrift store. It looks decent. The other second-grade teacher, Mrs. Okada, lent me a box of leveled readers she wasn't using. I mapped out the first unit already and I feel like I actually know what I'm doing for Monday. Haven't felt that way in a while. Small thing, but it matters. The commute is long and the parking lot has potholes, but I walked into that classroom and the kids were waiting and it just felt like where I was supposed to be.

Nudge: "Forty dollars on worksheets your first week — what made that feel worth it?"

Response: Because if I don't set this up right from the start, the kids lose momentum and I lose the year. I've seen it happen during student teaching. You can't get that first month back.

My old cooperating teacher from Edina called today to tell me they still have a position open. Full benefits, union contract, thirty kids max per class. She said she put in a word for me and they'd probably take me without a second interview.

I told her I'd think about it and then I sat in the parking lot for twenty minutes. Birch Lane doesn't even have a functioning copier half the time. But the literacy program here is the whole reason I came. I keep thinking about Mateo and the other kids who just started trusting me. I said no. I think I said no. I told her I'd call back Monday but I already know I'm not going to.

Nudge: "You sat there twenty minutes. What were you arguing with yourself about?"

Response: Whether I'm being stupid. Whether six months from now I'll wish I'd just taken the safe thing.

Barely slept. Kept going over that phone call. The Edina salary would have covered my student loans and left room to breathe. I know exactly what I'm giving up and I still can't make myself call her back.

Birch Lane sent around a memo about possible mid-year budget cuts. Nothing confirmed, but the newer hires would be first to go. That's me. Mrs. Okada told me not to worry, that they say this every fall, but she's been here eleven years so of course she's not worried.

Meanwhile my mom keeps forwarding me job postings from the county school district back home. Steady pay, pension, close to family. Every time she sends one I feel my chest tighten. She's not wrong — those jobs are real and they'd solve a lot of problems. But I keep picturing myself in a fluorescent classroom running a curriculum someone else wrote, and something in me just won't do it. I deleted the emails.

Nudge: "Your chest tightened. What does that feel like exactly?"

Response: Like I said, it's just stress. Everybody's stressed in September.

Had a normal day for once. Taught the short vowel unit, kids did okay on the pre-assessment. Ate lunch in the staff room instead of my classroom, which I almost never do. Talked to the PE teacher about the fall festival coming up. She asked if I wanted to run a booth and I said sure without even thinking about it. That felt easy for once.

After school I graded papers and reorganized the reading corner. Mateo finished his first leveled reader and asked if he could take the next one home. I said yes. His mom came to pick him up and thanked me for sending books home. She said he reads to his little brother at night now. That's the kind of thing that doesn't show up on any evaluation form but it's the whole point.

Drove home, made pasta, watched half a movie. Nothing remarkable. Just a day that went the way it was supposed to. Even the drive home felt calm. No knot in my stomach, no replaying conversations. Just a Tuesday.

Long week but I got through it. The reading groups are actually working. I can see the lower group catching up — two kids moved from level B to level D in three weeks. I stayed late Thursday to regroup the library baskets and it took forever, but now every kid can find a book at their level without asking me.

I'm tired but it's the right kind of tired. Like I did something that mattered today and I can feel it in my body. Haven't felt that way at a job before. Even when the copier jammed twice and I had to hand-write a worksheet on the whiteboard instead.

Nudge: "What does the wrong kind of tired feel like?"

Response: Like when I was student teaching in Edina and I'd come home drained but couldn't point to anything I'd actually built. Just following someone else's plan all day.
Fall festival was today. I ran the book swap booth. Kids brought in old books and traded them. Went fine. Cleaned up after and grabbed dinner with Mrs. Okada.

Ran the numbers this weekend. If I'd taken the Edina job I'd have an extra nine hundred a month after taxes. That's rent and then some. But money isn't the only thing that matters, and I'm tired of everyone acting like it should be.

Nudge: "Who's been saying that to you?"

Response: My mom, mostly. And my roommate from college who just got a salaried position downtown. She keeps asking how I'm "managing."

My mom drove up for the weekend and took me grocery shopping, which was nice until she started asking about my health insurance situation. I told her I'm on the school's plan and it's fine, which is technically true, but the deductible is high and I haven't been to the dentist since graduation. She got quiet in that way she does when she's holding something back.

On the drive home she finally said it. She said she worries about me, that I'm making things harder than they need to be, that there are good teaching jobs closer to home with real benefits and a pension. I know she's right about the math. I know the safer path is right there. But every time someone lays it out for me like that, something in me digs in harder. I don't want to build my life around avoiding the worst-case scenario. I want to build it around something I actually chose.

I didn't say that to her. I just said I'd look into it. But I'm not going to.

Nudge: "What did you actually choose when you picked Birch Lane?"

Response: I chose the kids. Isn't that enough?

Budget update came out. Birch Lane is keeping all current staff through the year. So that's settled, at least until spring. I should feel relieved but instead I just feel annoyed that I spent weeks worrying about it. The worry doesn't go away just because the thing you were worried about didn't happen.

My mom texted this morning asking if I'd thought about "what we talked about." I haven't replied yet. I know what the safer move looks like. I can list the reasons on paper. But I keep choosing this instead and I don't think that's going to change anytime soon. Maybe I'll feel differently when the next round of budget memos comes out, but right now I'm staying.

Sat with Amma on the phone for almost forty minutes after dinner, which I don't usually do on weekdays. She was telling me about the new water heater she got installed and how the plumber tried to overcharge her. I walked her through how to cross-check rates online, sent her a few links. She kept saying "you sound like your father" which — I don't know, it hit different tonight.

After we hung up I checked my bank balance, not because I needed to, just habit. Everything's where it should be. The SIP went through on the 10th, the rent auto-debit cleared, the FD interest got credited. There's something about seeing those numbers line up that lets me breathe properly. I know people think it's boring, the spreadsheets, the tracking, but when I see it all working the way I set it up, it's like — okay, things are holding together.

Spent the rest of the evening ironing my shirts for the week. Put on an old Kishore Kumar playlist. Just felt settled. Not every night is like this but tonight was.

Team review went well, Priya's onboarding tracker is actually working, the new hires are ramping faster than the last batch. I'd built the template she based it on, months ago, and watching it get adopted without anyone needing to ask me about it — that's the point, right? Systems that just run.

Renewed the apartment lease today, same terms, same landlord. He wanted to bump rent by two thousand but I showed him the market comparables I'd saved. He backed off. Walked home past the chai stall and the uncle remembered my order. Small thing but it's nice when the neighbourhood knows you.

Auto-debit for the health insurance premium bounced. Bank flagged insufficient funds because the salary credit was two days late this month. Sorted it manually but now I need to call the insurer to confirm coverage wasn't lapsed even briefly.

Nudge: "What's actually bothering you more — the bounce itself or the two days you couldn't control?"

Response: It's not about feelings, it's about process. If payroll is going to shift dates without notice, I need a bigger buffer in that account. That's the fix.

Took an Uber instead of the bus to a client meeting because I woke up late. Spent four hundred rupees on a fifteen-minute ride I normally do for thirty. Doesn't sound like much but I'd just tightened my monthly transport budget last week.

Nudge: "Four hundred rupees isn't the issue, is it?"

Response: It's not about the money specifically. It's that I had a plan and I broke it on day two. If I let small stuff slide the bigger stuff starts sliding too, that's just how it works.

The warehouse team flagged a potential compliance issue with one of our vendor contracts — some clause about liability thresholds that might not match the new state guidelines. I should've pulled the contract and reviewed it today but I had back-to-back calls and a quarterly deck to finish.

Told myself I'd get to it tomorrow but I've said that before about smaller things and sometimes tomorrow just becomes next week. The vendor's been fine for three years so it's probably nothing. But "probably" isn't really how I like to operate.

Still haven't looked at that vendor contract. It's been four days. Every morning I open my task list and it's there, highlighted yellow, and I just skip past it to whatever's on fire. Today it was a delivery routing issue that ate my entire afternoon.

The thing is, I know what I'd tell someone on my team — just block thirty minutes, pull the document, read the clauses, flag what needs flagging. It's not hard. But I keep not doing it and the longer I wait the more it feels like it's going to be a bigger problem than it actually is. Classic spiral.

Appa called tonight and asked about work. I said "all good, busy but managing." Which is mostly true. But I didn't mention the contract thing or the insurance bounce from last week, because then he'd worry, and then I'd have to manage his worry on top of everything else. I'm tired. Going to bed early and hoping tomorrow's lighter.

Noticed a crack in the bathroom wall tile near the water pipe. Could be nothing, could be a slow leak behind the wall. Decided to just keep an eye on it for now because calling the landlord means him sending his cousin who makes everything worse.

Nudge: "You'd normally be on the phone with the landlord by now."

Response: Yeah. I know. Last time his cousin came he left the tap fitting loose and I had a puddle for two days. But you're right, if it's actually a leak and I wait, the damage gets expensive. I'll message the landlord tonight and tell him I want a proper plumber this time, not his cousin.

Nothing much happening, which is fine. Work was the usual — approvals, a team standup, some back-and-forth with the Chennai office about a shipment delay that sorted itself out by lunch. Had dal chawal from the canteen, same as every Thursday because Thursday's dal is actually decent.

Walked to the ATM after work to deposit a cheque Amma sent for my birthday last month. I keep forgetting to do it, not because I need the money but because cheques feel like such an outdated thing. She could just UPI it but she likes writing cheques, says it feels more intentional. I stood in the queue behind an uncle who was arguing with the security guard about something, and I just waited. No rush.

Came home, wiped down the kitchen counter, checked that the gas knob was off — I always check twice — and sat on the balcony for a bit. The neighbours downstairs were playing some serial loudly. I could hear the dramatic music through the floor. Just one of those unremarkable evenings where everything is exactly where it should be and that's enough.

Nudge: "What does it feel like on the days when things aren't exactly where they should be?"

Response: Restless. Like there's an itch I can't locate. I just start going through things — did I lock the door, did I reply to that email, did the payment go through. Most of the time everything's fine but until I've checked, I can't sit still. It's not anxiety exactly, it's more like... I just need to know.
Vikram pitched me again on the startup thing over chai. Said they just closed a seed round and there's a spot for me, better title, equity. I said I'd think about it but I already know my answer.

Can't stop thinking about what Vikram said. Not the startup itself — I've made peace with not doing that, or I thought I had. It's the way he looked at me when I said I'd think about it. Like he felt sorry for me. Like staying put is the same as giving up.

And maybe from the outside it looks that way. He's out there raising money, hiring people, building something from scratch. I'm here making sure shipments arrive on time and vendor contracts don't have liability gaps. It's not glamorous. Nobody's writing articles about operations managers at logistics companies in Pune.

But then I think about what happens if his startup doesn't work out. He's burning through savings, he doesn't have insurance, his lease is month-to-month. One bad quarter and he's back at his parents' place in Nagpur. And I think about my FD, my insurance, my team that depends on me showing up every day with a plan — and I don't feel sorry for myself. I just feel different from him. But tonight it's harder to feel okay about that difference than it usually is.

Made pandan chiffon cake this morning. The kitchen smelled good for hours and I sat at the table eating a slice with hot Milo before anyone else was up. It was nice.

Nudge: "What changes when the house fills up?"

Response: Everything becomes about what needs doing next.

Same day, writing again. Wei Lin called this afternoon and asked me to watch the grandkids next Saturday because she has a work event. I said yes obviously. But it means I'll miss the CNY market at Chinatown with Mei and Hwee Leng. We've been planning it for two weeks.

It's fine. The kids love coming over and I already told Wei Lin I would. Mei said they can go without me and bring me back some bak kwa. I told her to get the thick-cut one from Lim Chee Guan.

Nudge: "Two weeks of planning is a lot to set aside."

Response: It is, but what am I going to say — no, find someone else, I want to go shopping? She's my daughter.

Spent the whole day with the grandkids as expected. They were fine, quite well behaved actually. But by 4pm I was so tired I could barely keep my eyes open. I sat down on the sofa for maybe ten minutes while they were watching cartoons and immediately felt guilty, like I should be doing something — cutting fruit, tidying up, checking if they needed water. So I got up.

Later after Wei Lin picked them up I heated some leftover soup and just stood in the kitchen eating it from the pot. Didn't even sit down. I wanted to take a long bath — the kind with the lavender salts my friend gave me for my birthday — but the bathroom still needed cleaning from the kids using it, and by the time I finished scrubbing I didn't feel like a bath anymore. I just showered and went to bed.

I keep thinking about the ten minutes on the sofa and how I couldn't even let myself have that. The children were fine. They were watching TV. Nothing was on fire. But something in me just wouldn't let it be okay to sit there.

Still tired from yesterday. Woke up with my neck stiff and couldn't really move it properly. Husband said I should go see the TCM doctor but I looked at the week ahead and there's grocery shopping tomorrow, Ma's checkup on Wednesday, and I told Mrs Tan I'd help with the community centre lunar new year cleanup on Thursday.

I know a massage would help. I know lying down with a heat pad for an hour would help. But I keep hearing my mother's voice in my head telling me not to be so pampered, that at her age she was carrying buckets of water uphill and still cooking for seven children. So I put Tiger Balm on it and kept going.

Finished the grocery run this morning and treated myself to a bowl of laksa at the hawker centre after. I almost didn't — the queue was long and I had frozen things in the bag. But I thought, five more minutes won't matter, and I stood in line anyway.

The laksa was really good. Extra hum. I sat at one of those metal tables by myself and didn't look at my phone or think about what was next. Just ate. By the time I was done the ice cream in the grocery bag was starting to soften but honestly it was worth it.

Nudge: "What tipped you into staying in that queue?"

Response: I could smell it. That's really all it was. The coconut milk and the chilli — I just thought, when was the last time I had this?

Valentine's Day, not that it matters to us at this age, but husband brought home a box of those nice mooncake-style pastries from Bengawan Solo after work. The tau sar ones I like. He just left them on the counter and didn't make a big deal about it which is his way.

I wanted to sit down and have one with tea and just enjoy it, the way you see people do in those café videos online. But I was in the middle of folding the laundry and then I remembered I hadn't called Ma today and she gets anxious if I don't call by 7, so I called her first, then finished the laundry, and by then I was standing at the counter eating the pastry over the sink because I was also rinsing out the thermos.

And the whole time I was eating it I was thinking about how I should be enjoying this more, which is such a strange thing to think, because the act of thinking it already means you're not. Like the pastry was good. I know it was good. I could taste it. But my mind was already three tasks ahead. I don't know when eating something nice became just another thing I do between other things. I saved the rest of the box for the grandkids.

Nudge: "You saved the box for the grandkids — was that automatic?"

Response: Yes. I didn't even think about it until you asked. Of course you give the nice things to the children. That's just what you do.

Went to Ma's nursing home today, usual Sunday visit. Brought her the dried tangerine peel she asked for and sat with her for two hours. She complained the aircon was too cold, same as always. I adjusted it, brought her an extra blanket, refilled her water thermos.

On the way home I stopped at the provision shop for dish soap and sponges. Cooked dinner. Washed up. Nothing unusual. I don't know why I'm even writing today, there's nothing to say really. Everything got done, nobody needed anything they didn't get. That should be enough.

Mei asked me last week if I wanted to join her for a foot reflexology session — the new place near Bishan that has the herbal soak. I said I'd think about it and I've been thinking about it for five days now. It's $38 for an hour. That's not a lot of money. We can afford it easily.

But there's this thing where I feel like I need to justify it first. Like I need to have done enough to deserve an hour of someone rubbing my feet. Which is ridiculous when I say it out loud. My husband never thinks twice about buying fishing lures that cost more than that. But for me it feels different. It feels like something I have to earn, and I'm never quite sure I have.

Went to the reflexology with Mei after all. It was good. My feet have been aching from all the standing and walking to the market so it actually made a difference. Mei and I talked the whole time about her daughter's wedding planning and it felt normal, like two friends just doing something together.

But on the MRT home I started thinking about the laundry I didn't hang up before leaving, and whether I'd left the rice cooker on, and I realised I'd chosen the useful thing to worry about instead of just sitting with the fact that I'd had a nice afternoon. The rice cooker was off. The laundry was fine. I just couldn't let the afternoon be what it was.

Nudge: "You caught yourself doing it this time."

The morning handover meeting. Dr. Fatima asked for an update on the new wound-care protocol I wrote up — before I could finish my second sentence, Khalid jumped in, started talking about the supply costs like he had been running the whole thing. He quoted numbers I had gathered. Used the phrase "what we've been looking at" when it was me who'd been looking at it, on my own time after shifts.

I just sat there. Let him finish. Dr. Fatima nodded along at him and moved to the next agenda item. Nobody looked at me. I don't know why I didn't say something. Maybe I was too tired, maybe it felt petty. But it's been sitting in my chest all day like a stone.

Nudge: "What stopped you from cutting in when Khalid started talking?"

Response: Honestly, I think I froze. It happened so fast and then the moment was gone. I keep telling myself it would have looked aggressive if I interrupted, but the truth is I just didn't react in time. And now it's too late.

I put in the request for the portable ultrasound unit three weeks ago. Three weeks. I have the justification, the cost breakdown, the vendor comparison — everything Procurement asked for. But it sits on Hassan's desk because he's "reviewing priorities." He told me he'll get to it when he gets to it.

So I wait. I check my email every few hours. I mentioned it to him again in the hallway on Thursday and he gave me this look, like I was being pushy. Maybe I was. But we need that unit for the patients on my ward. Every day without it is another day of sending patients downstairs for scans that take hours.

I called the vendor myself today to ask if there's a way to expedite, and they said it's all on our side. I know that. I already knew that. I just needed to do something because sitting still while someone else holds the cards is making me lose my mind. Layla says I bring the frustration home. She's probably right. Last night I snapped at Faisal over homework and it had nothing to do with him.

Nudge: "You said it had nothing to do with Faisal — what was it actually about?"

Response: It was about feeling stuck. When I can't move things forward at work, everything else starts to irritate me. Faisal just happened to be there asking me a question at the wrong time. I apologized to him later but I could see he was still careful around me the rest of the night.

Had to reorganize the shift schedule today after two nurses called in sick. Normally this kind of thing spirals — people complain, nobody wants to cover the gaps, and I end up doing half the work myself. But today I just pulled everyone into the break room, laid out what we had, and asked who could do what.

Something about the way I handled it felt right. I didn't raise my voice, didn't threaten anyone with write-ups like I've done before. Just said, here's the situation, we need to figure it out together. Nour volunteered to extend her shift and Omar swapped his day off without me having to ask twice. We got through it.

I'm exhausted now — twelve hours on my feet and barely ate. But there's this feeling I can't quite name. Like the ward ran well today because I kept it steady, not because I forced anything. I think the staff noticed too. Nour thanked me on her way out, said it was the calmest crisis she'd been through. Small thing, but I'm holding onto it.

People keep saying I should "let go" more at work, delegate, trust the team. Easy to say when you're not the one who gets called at 2am when something goes wrong. But fine — I gave Amira full responsibility over the medication audit this quarter. Didn't hover, didn't check in every day.

She came back with the report yesterday and it was thorough. Better organized than what I would have done, if I'm being honest. I told her it was solid work in front of the team. I meant it. She earned it. I'm not saying I'll start handing everything off, but maybe there are a few more things I don't have to hold onto so tightly.

The hospital announced new discharge protocols last week. I had my own version drafted, based on patterns I've been tracking on my ward for months — readmission rates, timing, which patients fall through the cracks. I brought it to the meeting with the clinical leads. Dr. Samir listened, then said the hospital would go with the standardized template from the regional office instead. Said it was about consistency across wards.

I pushed back. Told him the regional template doesn't account for the patient demographics we see. He acknowledged the point but said the decision was made. The rest of the table was quiet. I could tell Rania agreed with me but she didn't say anything. After the meeting she told me privately that I was right, which somehow made it worse.

So now I'm implementing a protocol I don't believe in. I went through it line by line this evening. It's not terrible. Some parts are fine. But the discharge timing section is going to cause problems and in three months when readmissions tick up, nobody will remember I flagged it. I printed out my version and put it in my drawer. Not sure why. Maybe just to know it exists.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to bring that drawer copy back out?"

Response: The drawer copy is just for me. If the numbers go where I think they will, the conversation will happen again on its own. I've been through this enough times.

New hire orientation today. I've been running this ward for seven years. The training coordinator introduced me to the group as "one of the senior nurses" — not the charge nurse, not the ward lead. Just "one of." I corrected her in front of everyone and I could tell she thought I was being difficult.

Then during the walkthrough, she explained the emergency tray setup to me. To me. Like I haven't been the one who redesigned it two years ago. I just nodded and moved on but I was grinding my teeth the whole time. I'm too tired to be angry about it but it's there, sitting under everything. Seven years and someone new walks in and I'm just "one of."

Staff meeting. We were discussing overtime allocation and I had the floor — I was explaining why my ward needed the extra hours, I had the patient acuity data right there on my tablet. Mid-sentence, Dr. Rashid starts talking over me to ask the administrator about budget ceilings. Just steamrolled right over me like I wasn't speaking.

Nobody said "Tariq was still talking." Nobody paused. The conversation just shifted. I sat there for the rest of the meeting barely saying a word. Drove home with my hands tight on the steering wheel. Called my brother Saeed — he said I should have spoken up. Everyone says that. It's easy to say when you're not the one sitting in that room being made invisible.

Nudge: "This is the second meeting where you went quiet. What would speaking up actually cost you?"

Response: I think I'm afraid they'll see me as the angry nurse who can't take being interrupted. In our department, if a doctor talks over you, that's just how it is. If I push back, suddenly I'm the problem. Saeed doesn't understand that — he works for himself. I don't have that option.

Submitted a request to attend the regional nursing leadership conference in Dubai next month. Need my department head's sign-off. Sent the form Monday, followed up Wednesday. He said he'd look at it. It's been two days since the follow-up and nothing.

Registration closes Friday. I've already arranged my own coverage for the shifts I'd miss. The conference fee is modest. There is no reason for this to take a week except that it's not his priority. So I wait.

Meeting with the nurse manager today about next year's staffing plan. She asked each charge nurse for input. When it was my turn, I laid out the case for an additional RN position on my ward — patient numbers, acuity trends, incident reports tied to understaffing. Clear, documented, took me two evenings to prepare.

She thanked me, then said the final staffing model would follow "organizational guidelines" and that individual ward requests would be "considered in context." Which means nothing. Which means someone above her will decide and my two evenings of preparation will sit in a folder.

What got me was watching her nod along to Ibrahim's request for the same thing on his ward — he barely had any data, just talked about how his team was "feeling stretched." She seemed more receptive to him. Maybe I'm reading into it. Maybe not. But I left that meeting knowing the decision was never going to be about the quality of the argument. It's about something else — relationships, politics, who plays golf with whom. And I don't play golf.

Nudge: "When you say it's not about the argument — what do you think it's actually about?"

Response: Access. Ibrahim has coffee with the nurse manager every week. They've known each other since she was a floor nurse. I came in from a different hospital system. I don't have that history. My work should speak for itself, but in this place, the work is just the entry ticket. The real decisions happen in conversations I'm not part of.

Still thinking about that meeting. The part that bothers me most isn't Ibrahim — it's that I spent two evenings on something and walked in believing the data would matter. I keep doing this. Building the case, presenting it perfectly, and then being surprised when it doesn't work.

Nudge: "You said you keep doing this — what would it look like to stop?"

Response: I don't think I can stop preparing. That's who I am. But maybe I need to start having the coffee meetings too. Play both sides of it. I just hate that it's necessary.

Zara had her eighteen-month checkup today. Doctor said weight and height are both tracking well. I'd been worried because Ammi kept saying she looks thin, that I'm not feeding her enough roti, the usual. The doctor didn't say any of that. She said the diet plan I put together is fine.

Came home, made aloo paratha for lunch, put Zara down for her nap. Farhan called during his break. He wants his cousin's family to come for dinner on Friday. I told him fine but I'm deciding the menu, not his mother. He laughed like I was joking. I wasn't.

Spent the afternoon editing two product review posts. One got decent engagement — forty-seven likes, twelve comments. Not much by any standard but the comments were real questions from other mothers asking my opinion on the brand. That part felt good.

So apparently Ammi decided to rearrange Zara's entire wardrobe while I was out getting groceries this morning. When I came back, all the drawers were different. She'd sorted everything by fabric weight — cotton in one drawer, winter things in another — and thrown out two outfits she decided were "too tight." One of them I'd bought last week.

I didn't even know what to say. Farhan was at work. I just stood in the nursery holding the grocery bags and looking at it. She came up behind me and said "I organized it properly, it was a mess." Like she was doing me a favor. Like the way I had it was wrong and she was correcting it.

I wanted to say this is my daughter's room, these are her clothes, and I decide what goes where. But what actually came out was "okay, thank you." Because what happens if I push back?
